sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content:
Good afternoon in Europe, good morning in the U.S. Welcome to our Q4 and fiscal year 2013 earnings conference call and webcast. Thank you very much to everyone joining the call. With us today is Mark Schneider, CEO of Fresenius; and Stephan Sturm, the CFO. 
Before we get started, I'd like to mention that today's call is being recorded. And I'd also like to draw your attention to the forward-looking statement and disclaimer wording on Page 2 of the presentation. And this cautionary language also applies to the presentation and comments we may make today. Mark Schneider will now start with a general overview and Stephan Sturm will take you through the financials. Afterwards, management is prepared to answer your questions. 
Now I would like to hand it over to Mark. Mark, please?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content:
Thank you, Birgit, and a warm welcome to our conference call participants today. As always, we appreciate your interest in our company. It was a busy and yet another successful year for Fresenius, with a lot of progress regarding some of our key operating and strategy objectives. 
I know from your feedback over the morning that our outlook has caused disappointment among our investors. Stephan and I will be happy to allow extra time for your questions in this call. We will get to our outlook later, but let me say up front that our guidance is driven by the notion that in business, delivering the goods is more important than promising them. 
Birgit has pointed out the Safe Harbor language to you, so let's move right to Page 3, which puts our 2013 net sales and net income development in a 10-year perspective. We set new records on both metrics in 2013, and achieved our goal of exceeding EUR 20 billion in sales and EUR 1 billion in net income, 1 year ahead of plan. As you can see from the timeline, it has been a rewarding decade for Fresenius. And our new midterm goal for 2017, which I will cover later, shows that our prospects continue to be bright. 
Page 4 confirms that shareholders are fully participating in these strong financial results. For the year 2013, we will propose a dividend increase of 14% to our Board of Directors and to our shareholders in the Annual General Meeting in May. This increase is fully in line with the dividend policy we outlined last year. Under this policy, we intend to grow dividends in lockstep with earnings growth before onetime items. As we pursue this policy, the payout ratio is expected to stabilize and slightly over 20%. The chart on the right-hand side confirms that our compounded annual total shareholder returns of the last 10 years exceeds the DAX average by about 10 percentage points. 
Let's turn now to Page 5, and to specific financial results for this year. In 2013, we achieved sales of EUR 20,331,000,000, EBIT of 3,045,000,000 and net income of EUR 1,051,000,000. Growth rates and actual currency rates were a little below constant currency growth rates, as both the U.S. dollar and some key emerging market currencies weakened compared to the year 2012.
When it comes to EBIT growth, please note that the impact coming from Fresenius Medical Care gets overstated in our consolidated financial results. Fresenius Medical Care's EBIT gets fully consolidated in those numbers, while net income reflects just the 31% share of earnings that we have in Fresenius Medical Care. Excluding Fresenius Medical Care, our EBIT growth for the full year 2013 was 7% in actual currency and 8% in constant currency. 
Let's turn to Page 6 and the financial results by business segment. Sales growth was strong across the board, and, in fact, double-digit for Fresenius Kabi and Fresenius Vamed. Fresenius Helios was leading EBIT growth with a 21% increase, marking the eighth consecutive year of double-digit EBIT growth. EBIT growth at Fresenius Medical Care and Fresenius Kabi was impacted by several extraordinary items, including U.S. sequestration; the label change for our blood volume expander, HES; FDA remediation costs; and the price cuts in China. As you will see from the detailed business unit presentations and our expectations for 2014 and beyond, most of these items were either stabilized over time or not recurrent. 
Before turning to Kabi, Helios and Vamed in greater detail, a brief word on Fresenius Medical Care. The company is making good progress in the face of significant reimbursement changes in 2013 and 2014. I applaud CMS for advanced rebasing rule, which was published in late November 2013. But there's also no denying that flat U.S. reimbursement requires us to step up our efforts to improve efficiency. The combination of ongoing efficiency improvements and Dialysis' growth prospects outside of the established markets in the U.S. and Western Europe, should result in solid earnings growth prospects for Fresenius Medical Care in the midterm. 
Let's turn now to Fresenius Kabi. The company has performed at the top end of our expectations in 2013 in spite of several adverse issues, in particular, the China price cuts and the sales reduction for our blood volume expander, HES. With continuing drug shortages in the important U.S. market, we achieved organic sales growth of 5%. To get a good understanding of our organic sales growth expectations for 2014, Slide 7 provides a helpful breakdown by geography. 
For Europe and the emerging markets, we expect improvement over 2013, as some of the 2013 items are stabilizing. When it comes to Europe and HES, in particular, let me confirm that sales development in the fourth quarter of 2013 was in line with our earlier projections. We saw a Q4 decrease of about 40% year-over-year and a decrease of about 20% to 25% for the full year 2013. We now expect that sales would stabilize at the Q4, 2013 levels in 2014. 
For North America and the drug-shortage situation, we are faced with the same issue that we saw in the past 4 years. We simply cannot predict how the situation develops. The I.V. drug shortage situation at the end of January was roughly unchanged from what we saw in the fourth quarter of 2013. Meanwhile, new shortages have occurred, such as the one for large volume I.V. fluids. FDA has granted regulatory discretion to allow Fresenius Kabi to import various sizes of sodium chloride solutions from Europe. We are currently developing a timeline to begin supplying limited quantities of these solutions to the United States.
Another important marker is our Propofol market share in the U.S. We exited the year with a combined Propofol and Diprivan market share of 88%, despite compensation from 3 other generics. Our January shipments of Propofol confirmed strong, underlying demands, and we expect to retain majority share for this product for the year 2014. When it comes to I.V. drug launches in the U.S., we came in, as expected, below our annual target of 6 to 10. We launched 3 products in 2013, and also expect to be slightly below our 6 to 10 launch target in 2014. 
The good news is that our launch schedule then foresees a bolus of launches in the 2015 through 2017 time frame, when we expect to exceed our 6 to 10 targets in some or maybe all of these years. All of these items I just described for the year 2014 are fully reflected in our Kabi guidance, which Stephan will share with you later this call. 
Before turning to Kabi's midterm prospects, a brief update on our quality efforts. This is a key issue for us. We have devoted significant top management attention and investment through remediating of the situation in our Grand Island, Kalyani and Maricao plants. 
We also strengthened the Kabi quality assurance organization. At this stage, we're still awaiting the next FDA audit in Grand island. We are also restarting our Kalyani facility. And we have been audited just again recently by FDA in Maricao with 0483 issued at the close-out meeting, reflecting the strong remediation efforts made at the site. 
Let's turn now to the midterm prospects of Fresenius Kabi on Page 8. We believe that those prospects are very attractive. They are, in particular, driven by continued growth opportunities in the generic I.V. drugs space, and by broadening and fully rolling out our product portfolio in emerging markets. Recent emerging markets' weakness may have affected the currency situation, but we have not seen any material impact on the health care spending that these emerging economies are targeting. Without going into the details of all the countries listed in the table on the right-hand side of Page 8, please note that we have plenty of room to grow our product spectrum in each of these markets.
China is a case in point. Our 2013 sales in China were about EUR 500 million, almost 3/4 of that revenue came from our leading position in the medical nutrition market that we enjoy in China, roughly 1/4 came from all the other Kabi product groups. As you compare that to the global Kabi revenue breakdown by product group, it is exactly the other way around: about 1/4 medical nutrition, 3/4 the other Kabi product groups. As to build our position in the Chinese market, we will be striving for a more balanced revenue composition by strongly expanding the sales in these other product groups. 
With this, let me turn to Page 9 and Helios. This slide recaps the status of our major project, which is the acquisition of 40 clinics from Rhön-Klinikum. The graph on the right-hand side shows you the combined locations, which will give us unprecedented geographic coverage in Germany. While antitrust approval took a little longer than anticipated, we will be able to consolidate revenues and earnings of about 70% of the acquired business as of January 1, 2014. For about 20% of the acquired business, the consolidation date will be March 1. For 2 hospitals in the cities of Wiesbaden and Salzgitter, approval of municipal shareholders is still pending. At this stage, we are fully confirming our published estimates for synergies, integration costs and earnings accretion.
Turning to Vamed on Page 10. Vamed has had a stellar year, with 21% sales growth. The company also reached its EUR 1 billion sales target a full year ahead of plan. With order entry in the project business at an all-time high, we are also looking with confidence towards the future. As you can see from the graph on the right-hand side, the company has cautiously shifted its project and service business mix from roughly 70/30 to less than 60/40 over the past 5 years. This all happened at a time when we saw double-digit project business growth. With the service contracts, we bring more stability to the top line since they are typically multiyear. This helps to decrease sales and earnings volatility.
Stephan will cover the details 2014 guidance for the group in the key business segments in his part of the presentation, but I wanted to give you a sense of where we are headed midterm through the year 2017. Our new stretch target calls for about EUR 30 billion in sales and net income in the EUR 1.4 billion to EUR 1.5 billion range. The revenue target will include, first and foremost, organic sales growth, but also some small- to medium-sized acquisitions. As we reach our midterm targets for each of the business segments, we will update those periodically as well. With our strong and balanced health-care portfolio and our focus on solid operational improvements, we're certainly headed in the right direction. 
This concludes my part of the presentation. At this point, let me hand it over to Stephan for a detailed look at that financials.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content:
Thank you, Mark. Good afternoon, good morning, welcome, thanks for joining. Last year, at this point, we said that 2013 would be another record year and it was. So straight over to the group P&L, and that's on Slide 13.
Q4 sales came in at EUR 5.3 billion, up 6% year-on-year at constant currency. Reported growth is 2%, so with minus 4 points, we again, had a meaningful currency headwind in the past fourth quarter, albeit at the lower end of my guidance. 
And to reiterate my statement from our Q3 call, that is primarily, but not exclusively, due to U.S. dollar weakness. We continue to look at meaningful currency devaluations in Venezuela, Argentina, South Africa, India, but also Japan. Again, all relatively minor revenue contributions, but it adds up. 
For the full year, as for the right-hand column, we're at plus 8.4% at constant currency, so bang in the middle of our 7% to 10% guidance. And as projected during the last earnings call, a currency effect for the full year of minus 3 points. 
Organic sales growth for the group reached 4.5%, also in line with my guidance of around 5%. EBITDA, not on this slide, with the margin reached 20.1% in the fourth quarter, up 1 point quarter-on-quarter, with improvements across all business segments. 
Depreciation/amortization of 4.2% in the fourth quarter, stable from Q3, slightly above our traditional 4% level, that's given the amortization charges from recent acquisitions, Fenwal in particular. Also for 2014, I'd expect a ratio of just above 4%.
Q4 EBIT was EUR 843 million, up 2% year-on-year at constant currency. And given stability on D&A, the EBIT margin is also up 1 point from Q3 to 15.9%. That took the full year margin to 15% flat. Corporate EBIT was minus EUR 25 million in the full year. That amount includes EUR 6 million from Fresenius Biotech. For 2014, adjusted for Biotech, I'd expect no meaningful change and, therefore, around minus EUR 20 million. 
On to net interest, which was EUR 135 million in Q4, very much in line with the prior quarter and that took the full year to EUR 584 million versus my guidance of approximately EUR 600 million, or slightly below. 
Going forward, I believe that Q3 and Q4 can serve as a good proxy for the underlying run rate. EUR 135 million times 4 takes you to EUR 540 million. Now we've got to add the Rhön financing expenses and there, I'd assume approximately 3.5% on roughly a EUR 3 billion purchase price. That will get you to around another EUR 100 million on top. Application of free cash flow over the course of the year will reduce the aggregate number slightly, so for 2014, I expect the net interest charge of EUR 610 million to EUR 630 million. 
Taxes, well, the group tax rate improved further to 27.8% in the full year. That level, in my mind, is not sustainable, unfortunately, even when taking into account the well-below-average tax rate of the Rhön hospitals. For 2014, therefore, I expect a slight increase to up to 29%. 
Finally, net income, and that was EUR 298 million in the fourth quarter, up 21% year-on-year at constant currency, and that acceleration of growth took the full year rate to 14%, the top end of our guidance range. And as per my guesstimate from the third quarter, the full year foreign exchange impact was minus 2 points. Also, incidentally for the group, excluding FMC, Q4 net income growth was a mighty 26%, or 20% at constant currency in the full year. So once again, very decent growth rates off a demanding base. 
With that, let's move over to Kabi on Slide 14. And as usual, when it comes to the EBIT margin, let's start at the bottom of that page. And there, you can see total EBIT, excluding Fenwal, of EUR 901 million. A strong fourth quarter margin of 20.5% took the full year to 19.8%. So that's in the upper quarter of our guidance range. One row up, Fenwal contributed to EUR 25 million in the full year to a total of EUR 926 million, diluting Kabi's margin to 18.5%, right in the middle of our guidance range. And the minus 1% growth year-on-year actually turns into a plus 1% on a constant currency basis. 
Fenwal's negative EBIT in the fourth quarter is a true one-off. In the planned move, we canceled some contracts with external contributes to gain even more control over our destiny. And as a consequence, we have to reverse prior revenues and EBIT. Much more importantly, we're making good progress integrating the business, and synergies have started to come through as planned. And also, organic sales growth is well in line with our expectations. 
On to FDA remediation charges, and those are EUR 31 million in the full year. That is relative to our guidance of up to EUR 50 million, net of the book gain on the disposal of our French Calea business. I can tell you know that, that book gain amounts to approximately EUR 50 million. So the FDA remediation costs is approximately EUR 80 million gross in 2013.
Now that's a massive amount and let me clarify that we've taken a very comprehensive and, thus, conservative approach on this. The gross amount includes both cash and non-cash items, so we're looking at forgone EBIT relative to earlier expectations, as well as the value of scrapped inventories, just as much as we include consulting fees, penalties and higher sourcing costs for external APIs. Given this wide definition, our original guidance remains valid. We continue to believe in a total amount of up to EUR 50 million net of the book gain. So we expect some further expenses this year and potentially next, but this is fully reflected in our guidance.
Now on to the regions from top to bottom. In Europe, I have guided to a meaningful margin pickup in Q4 on the back of I.V. drug launches and transfusion tenders, and that actually materialized, 19.3% in Q4, that took the full year margin to 17.4%. For this year, I'd expect a margin at around last year's level. On the one hand, we see underlying progress, but that will be offset, at least partially, by missing EBIT from HES sales. 
I had cautioned you about Q4 in North America. There, a margin of 32.1% took the full year to 35.9%, so in line with my mid-30s guidance. Key drivers of the quarter-on-quarter decrease were the Fenwal topic that I just mentioned, higher R&D spending, but also a few onetimers. For 2014, Mark described the uncertainties surrounding drug shortages and the margin range that goes along with a fairly wide range for organic growth spans from the low- to the mid-30s. And that, to be clear, corresponds to a range from the mid- to the high-30s for just our I.V. drugs business. 
Q4 margin in the emerging markets was a bit behind our expectations. I had hoped for 17% in the full year, rather than the actual 16.5%. But in particular, given the unabated strong volume growth, we're confident of a margin pickup in 2014. We are having a bit of a spillover from the price cuts in China, but, on the other hand, the lower FDA remediation charge in Kalyani is going to help.
The absolute margin level, that will still be impacted by HES and, just mentioned, the China's price cuts and the mixed effect, whereas a few countries with above-average margins suffer from currency devaluation and enter the mix with a lower rate. Corporate and R&D saw the benefit of the Calea book gains last year. The underlying growth stems from higher R&D expenses. But I'd view the adjusted 2013 level also as a good proxy for this year.
Those were my comments on Kabi, let's move on to Helios on Slide 15, where we reached full year sales of almost EUR 3.4 billion, up 6% year-on-year. Organic sales growth was again a bit softer in the fourth quarter. But with 3%, we made our guidance. And going forward, we don't see any structural impediment, rather the contrary. Total EBIT in the full year was EUR 390 million, up 21% year-on-year, and close to the top end of our guidance range. That's a margin of 11.5%, up a further 40 basis points from the 9-month level, driven by the excellent Q4 performance of 12.6%. 
Over to Helios' clinic development plan on Slide 16. And before we go into the details. This is the new methodology we announced last August, so we're showing EBIT, rather than EBITDA. It's 6, rather than 5 years to maturity. And importantly, we're incorporating post-acute percentages, which are, in general, margin-dilutive. 
Now into the table. As every year on this occasion, let's start with the total column on the very right. And there, you have the calculated EBIT target of EUR 337 million, and below the actual results of EUR 387 million. So also for this year, mission accomplished. The development plan works. 
Moving to the very left, you can see where the bulk of the over achievement accrues, it's in the year 1 cluster, to which we have allocated the Damp clinics. Now you may say that's not entirely fair, as those hospitals were already privatized when we acquired them. On the other hand, that is how we've always done it, this is no change of methodology. And importantly, even if you stripped out the overachievement in year 1, we make the overall target. So the plan actually works. Now the Rhön hospitals, given their aggregate mass, actually called for an adjustment to the methodology. And so for 2014, we will allocate them to the full year bracket, with an 8% EBIT market -- EBIT margin target. 
On to the individual age classes. Year 1, Damp, we've covered already. Year 2, a shortfall, but an EBIT swing of EUR 6 million year-on-year. Year 3, also a shortfall, but also an improvement year-over-year. Year 5 is characterized by hospitals that are just pre the finalization of major construction projects, and that is the explanation for profitability going sideways. Year 6, target reached, with a year-over-year EBIT increase of 20%. The majority cluster, also target reached. We're not quite happy with the 17:31 ratio you see at the bottom, but that is what we're going to be working on, and that is actually why we revised the plan last summer to make it more ambitious. 
Briefly onto cash flow on Slide 17, which was, again, very solid in the past fourth quarter. Let's start with the group at the bottom. Operating cash flow of EUR 754 million, versus EUR 619 million in Q3 and EUR 503 million in the second quarter. Agreed, the full year margin of 11.4% is slightly below the previous year, but you will recall that 2012 includes cash from the collection of overdue payments in Spain and Portugal, and also some extraordinary items from Vamed's project business.
CapEx at 5.1% is well in line with our expectations. And consequently, free cash flow, nicely above 6% for the full year. For the individual businesses, very strong cash collections at FMC, also a very decent quarter at Helios, with a year-on-year DSO improvement. I'm still not entirely happy with inventories at Kabi, but we're in the process of taking care of that. And for Vamed, we actually saw the strong finish I have guided you to with a positive operating cash flow full year.
As for 2014, for the group, we expect to achieve an operating cash flow of 9% to 11% -- 10% to 11%. CapEx guidance comes in at approximately 6% of sales. So as in the past -- and you may recall the slide that I have shown you last year on this occasion -- we're looking at free cash flow in the mid-single digit. On that basis, we're also guiding you fully incorporating the closing of the Rhön transaction to a net debt to EBITDA in a range between 3 and 3.25. 
Over to the guidance for Fresenius Kabi, where Mark has already pointed out to you what the key driver of the organic growth is. You'll see at the bottom of that page, it is the ongoing uncertainty with regard to the I.V. drug market shortage in North America. And also, if you put together everything that I said about my expectations for the individual regions within Kabi, you should arrive at a margin for Kabi of 16% to 18%. The upper end, basically indicating no change from the drug shortage situation that is prevailing right now. The 16% would foresee a significant easing of that structural situation. 
Now some of you have asked why even the upper end of that margin range is below what we're seeing in 2013. Now first of all, there are already some indications that the drug shortage situation has eased relative to where we were 1 year ago. But second and thirdly, you also see the points at the bottom of that page. It is, in particular, the full year effect of missing HES sales and EBIT, and the full year effect of the price cuts in China.
Over the page, on Helios, where at this point in time we prefer only to give a guidance for Helios as it stands right now. We have a lot of respect for guiding you and, as Mark indicated earlier, we really want to make sure that we deliver the goods. And therefore, we would like to be the proper owners of these hospitals before we give you a hard solid, reliable guidance. We also have a lot of respect for our colleagues at Rhön, and, clearly, do not want to front run their communication to the capital markets, which is due only about 2 weeks from now. 
For the existing Helios portfolio, as in prior years, we do forecast 3% to 5% organic growth. Where we're coming from is never change a winning guidance, but I would readily admit that this incorporates a cautious stance on organic patient growth, given that we have a higher price inflated than in the prior years and given that we also have the support from the federal aid package. We actually had a decent start to the year as far as organic patient growth is concerned, so it remains to be seen whether that growth guidance will prove to be too cautious. 
As far as the EBIT guidance is concerned, of EUR 390 million to EUR 410 million, some of you have already asked me why there isn't more progress relative to what we're showing in 2013, what you need to incorporate is the 2 hospitals that we had to dispose of in order to make the Rhön acquisition work in Borna and Zwenkau, they would have contributed an EBIT of between EUR 10 million and EUR 15 million this year. So that is what you have to adjust for. Then you're going to arrive at underlying EBIT growth.
Let me confirm what Mark said, for 70% of the Rhön transaction we have accomplished consolidation as -- from January 1. And whilst we will only come back to you at a later occasion, probably as part of a quarterly earnings call, with a firm quantitative guidance, I want to echo what Mark just said and we would like to fully, qualitatively reconfirm our prior statements on synergies, integration costs and EPS accretion. 
As far as Vamed is concerned, notwithstanding their strong performance in 2013, also they made their midterm target 1 year ahead of time, we believe in ongoing growth as far as sales and EBIT is concerned. The 5% to 10% is the growth rate that we continue to see, unabated, unchanged from what we were telling you 1 year ago.
Put all these together on Page 20 for the group, 12% to 15% constant currency growth. That is driven by organic growth in the mid-single digits. Plus, first and foremost, the contribution that we expect the Rhön hospitals to make. That's going to take us to 12% to 15% revenue growth. Net income growth, for the reasons that I mentioned earlier, in particular the certainty at Kabi and the I.V. drug shortages in North America, there, we do foresee 2% to 5%. 
As far as the midterm targets are concerned, some of you asked about the implied growth rate. And I'd say, the proper way, from our perspective, to look at this is to take 2014 as the starting point. And if you do that, then you will aim -- arrive at fairly similar growth rates for top and bottom line, and you will also arrive at bottom line growth rates that are in the very high single digits, if not stretching to the double digits. I believe that given the critical mass that we have put together, that is still a highly attractive growth rate. I also want to remind you that on the last 2 occasions, when we put out medium-term guidance goals, we achieved them ahead of time and, therefore, that is going to be -- whilst we call that a stretched target, that is going to be an ambitious push for us to make that work.
That concludes my part of the presentation. Mark and I will now be happy to take your questions. Thank you.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: If I could start with Kabi and your growth guidance, Stephan. You talk a lot about the uncertainty in the I.V. generic market in the U.S., and that has certainly been clearly an uncertain market in terms of guiding to that market for many years. Given that your share in Propofol has held up so well, given that there has been no obvious change in market growth as you yourself have said in the early months of this year compared to the fourth quarter, and that's what we hear from other companies as well, can you give us more color about what new uncertainty you are seeing to sort of get us, say, to minus 5%? That will be my first question.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Ed, this is Mark. Maybe I can take the first crack at this. I think the fundamental difference here between the I.V. generics and the other Kabi product groups is how quickly that can swing around when product becomes available in the market and you've got a choice. And compared to the 3 other product groups, what we've seen time and time again is that the I.V. generic market gyrates up and down much more violently. And we have had perfect storms. The one that I remember was Q2 2009, when in a quick, short order of time, we saw things turn worse. And we weren't used to that sort of forecasting at the time yet, because our I.V. fluids and nutrition business are so much more stable. And I think we didn't repeat that mistake and we certainly have no intention of repeating it again. So for forces that are not under our control, I think we have no choice but to take a cautious view, and then basically share with you the latest insight as we go through the year and adjust our guidance when needed.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Ed, let me add that this is clearly more than just Propofol, and that this is a fairly broad portfolio of individual drugs that our success is built on. And you will have heard that from me before. We continue to hold the view that we must not second guess what our competitors are saying about how long it's going to take them to get their problems fixed, yes? And then we're working on that basis. That is what the model is built on, and that implies a bit of caution.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: No -- absolutely, that's very clear. But, I mean, is there any specific other products or product launches from competitors that you are concerned about that you would want to highlight?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Ed, there is no specific knowledge we have at this present time that we have ahead of you. So again, this is caution. But let me also put this into a wider context and that is, even those good years when we saw very, very nice growth rates coming from the shortages, we warned you that there was going to be a time when this will normalize again, and that this time will imply more cautious year-over-year comparable and that the underlying fundamental story is not shortages. In fact, if we had the choice, we would prefer for no shortages to be there, because clearly, for the patients it's better to have no shortages. What our underlying story was getting new products approved and launching them. And I think, over time, what we need to do is segue back to a situation where this is what's driving our growth. And hence, I think the data point I gave you earlier that we're expecting a bolus of new applications and approvals and launches in the '15 to '17 time frame, I think that, to me, is a more fundamental and more important point than a specific quarter on a specific drug that may be in shortage.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: No -- absolutely. In that case, my follow-up question would be, if the thing is things we need to look at it in terms of Fenwal. Because, Stephan, to your guidance, that you still expect mid to high margins in the I.V. generics business, in the mid to high 30s, which is what I would've expected in terms of a gradual decline in the margin there. In looking at the first quarter and your comments on that is, could you maybe quantify in more detail the impacts of the Fenwal reversals on the fourth quarter margin?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Q4 saw negative EBIT from Fenwal, so it was a -- that is what I said, and therefore, we had a very meaningful effect on the margin, pushing it down to the around 32% that I mentioned. But that is a true one-off. On the other hand, going forward, in particular given the amortization charge -- you heard that on prior quarters on quarterly calls, notwithstanding the underlying progress that we're making, and what is going to have a dilutive effect.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: And, Ed, let me just take that opportunity to build on what Stephan just said. I know there were some nervousness in the market surrounding this business, and this came back to some competitors commenting on specific tenders, which is not our policy to do. And let me just say, we are very happy with how this integration is going and with how the business is going. So again, as Stephan pointed out, the fourth quarter situation clearly is part of our integration and is part of upgrading that business over time as we have guided you to expect. And whatever disappointment you may be seeing here in our expectations for 2014, is not primarily driven by Fenwal.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: So I had 2 on Helios and 1 on the medium-term guidance. I was just wondering with Helios, whether you could give us a bit more color on what drove the slowdown in inorganic growth in Q4, given the favorable reimbursement changes. I guess, it was volumes, but some color there will be helpful. The second question in Helios relates to the 17/31 split of hospital above and below target for Helios in the 6-year-and-longer group. As a group, that portfolio of hospitals is within the 12% to 15% margin target. So is it that there are just a lot of smaller hospitals that are sort of sub-margin, or there are couple of big hospitals which need to be turned around or improved on to really swing the margins there. I'm just getting a feel for what kind of level of incremental EBITs you could possibly generate out of those 31 facilities that have been around for a while, but is still below par over, perhaps, the next 1 to 2 years. And then I'll ask the second question in a second.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Tom, this is Mark. Let me start on the organic growth question, then hand it over to Stephan for the margin model. I think you're right. In light of the inflator that we saw and also that special package that kicked in August 1, organic growth was a little soft in the second half of 2013. To our knowledge, this is mostly driven by less growth on the case numbers than we had anticipated. We have seen this not just in our hospitals. We've seen these elsewhere as well. And we're still trying to understand it. However, we're not aware of any permanent trend here. And so -- in fact, as Stephan pointed out, January was quite satisfactory on that front, and we're still trying to understand it better. There was some concern in the German market about specific procedures and whether those are overprescribed, if you will. I'm not sure whether that's driving it. There's really nothing here that we believe that is really permanently reducing the case numbers. In fact, if you look at demographics, you should expect the case numbers over time will hold up and grow.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Sure. Okay, that's helpful.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: And, Tom, maybe to add to that, really, we haven't found a good explanation for it. That's why I said nothing structural. It can be something as mundane as their winter and no real flu season, or it can be the exact location of the Christmas holidays. But notwithstanding that, the absence of a valid explanation, we have taken a more cautious stance on patient growth, as I mentioned when I was talking about Helios' guidance. On your second question, Tom, the 17 to 31, I really would like to turn that on its head by saying that is the reason why we revised our methodology. That is, at least in the very first year, the effect of including the post-acute facilities, yes? And therefore, as we mentioned, just structurally come at a lower margin. You shouldn't expect those to reach the 15%. But we really -- we'll be trying hard to get many, most, not all, but most of them into that 12% cluster.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Okay. And any idea, roughly, if you could share with us where they are now in terms of the post-acute care and how much of the current revenue base that -- those specifically account for.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Give me at least the fig leaf, okay, here.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Fair enough. Oh, and final question was just sort of more of a conceptual one on this EUR 30 billion 2017 revenue goal. I mean, from a base of EUR 20 billion this year, I guess, EUR 2 billion of that additional EUR 10 billion comes from Rhön, roughly. Of the remaining EUR 8 billion, would you be prepared to give us at least some feel of how much of that is likely to come organically and how much from acquisitions across the business?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Tom, we can't give a precise breakdown, but I think we are stressing that organic growth is the cornerstone of making our targets and that acquisitions, as you know, looking at Helios when they're privatizing hospitals or copy-making small, tuck-under acquisitions, or FMC acquiring new dialysis centers, I mean, those occur all the time. But the key number that we watch is organic growth and we see no sign of that really weakening over time significantly.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Okay. So would it be fair to assume that, that target includes the usual sort of stream of tuck-ons that you've done pretty much every year for time immemorial, but doesn't include a thing on the very substantial Rhön, APP sort of scale.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Absolutely. And as those things happen, you may recall from the past, our technique was always the same and that is we take the deal, we size it up and we add it to the target. So this is small- to mid-sized acquisitions that are part of our acquisition routine. If there's big stuff coming, we'll adjust the target.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: I'd like to start off just by looking at the Kabi tailwinds and headwinds. And, Stephan, I appreciate there are a lot of moving parts. But can you us understand the size of the China headwind and the HES headwind that you have in mind as you think about 2014?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Look, in round numbers, that China is a EUR 500 million market. We were indicating that the price cut was 8% to 10%, yes, and it kicked in, in the month of February.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: This is February 2013 or February 2014?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: It kicked in February of '13. That's why we have the annualization effect of a good month in 2014.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Understood. And in terms of HES?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: In terms of HES, we were saying that it only -- it all happened over the course of the first quarter. We rapidly lost sales and EBIT over the course of Q2 and Q3, even more in Q4, but we believe that we've reached the bottom in Q4.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Okay. But simplistically looking at this, you have upside from remediation, upside from Fenwal, if I have done my math correctly, those imply your upside of the EUR 80 million-or-so in terms of EBIT and you have it offset by 5 to 25 in terms of the tailwinds -- the headwinds. I'm just trying to understand why the EBIT guidance for Kabi is not greater than what you've provided. And correct my math if I've made a mistake there somewhere.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Your math was a bit too fast for me, Veronika. I may have to look at that a bit separately. But I mentioned 3 factors. Yes, it is HES, but the profitability of that product mustn't be underestimated. It is the China effect that I just mentioned. And the third factor that I mentioned is that relative to where we were 1 year ago, we've already seen a bit of easing in the U.S. structural regions.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Okay, understood. And can you comment a little bit on your medium-term guidance expectations for Kabi is 18% to 21%? Given that, in theory, you might be looking at a margin as low as 16% this year, is that something that you still believe in?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Look, Veronika, the 18% to 21% are formally still out, but we haven't -- we have not adjusted that margin target for the arrival of Fenwal, where, as Mark said earlier, this is a structurally more stable but also margin-dilutive business. It is not adjusted for the aim to grow even more in medical devices, where the same metrics do apply. I also want to caution you that even at the heydays of drug shortages, we never went beyond the 21%. So bear with us until we see a bit clearer on drug shortages. Bear with us until we are a bit clearer on medical devices and then we'll give you a proper update for the midterm target for Fresenius Kabi. But the 16% that clearly -- at the bottom end of our range, that clearly should not be seen as an indication for the midterm for Fresenius Kabi.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content:
Jerry, can you hear me? Any further questions?
[Technical Difficulty]

sections.speaker: Alexander Kleban
sections.content: So just 3 questions for me. So the first one is just in the 2017 guidance, can you give any sense of what your -- what assumptions you're taking for medical care within that in terms of Medicare rates and cost management? And then I'll do the next to you after that, if that's okay.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes. We have not provided a specific breakdown by business segment of that midterm target, but, of course, it fully incorporates what CMS have legislated and announced in November. And -- so we're basically working from that.

sections.speaker: Alexander Kleban
sections.content: Okay. And then on acquisitions, just, so when do you think you'll be ready to do another big strategic deal, sort of a Rhön-sized deal? I mean, clearly want to digest Rhön first, so how many years, do you think, it would take to get that process done and when might you be ready for something big again?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Well, as a matter of policy, we prefer to announce those deals after the fact when they're concluded, so it's kind of hard to point to one. But I think unlike small to midsize deals that really occur as a standard business process overtime, these hard ones are hard to plan for. And what counts for us more than a big deal is that on the existing deals, we do a solid integration job, deliver the financial targets that we're committing to and making sure the whole thing works. And so we don't plan those. We're not in a rush to do anything new. I mean, keep in mind, we're just 2 days from closing the last big one. Closing is anticipated to occur this Thursday when it comes to those Rhön hospitals. And so if this is one massive integration job we have ahead of ourselves for later this year, it would certainly be premature now to basically race to the next target. And its -- right now, I mean, we've got to focus on just strong operational performance, a good integration job, good organic growth.

sections.speaker: Alexander Kleban
sections.content: Okay. Very clear things. And then just last one, it's probably more housekeeping, but on the Rhön -- on the onetime cost and the synergies, is there any change on the phasing for that? Or any more visibility in phasing, given when you close the deal and just for us thinking about the quarters as we go ahead this year?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: In all likelihood, the share of onetime expenses is, given the delayed closing, going to fit a bit more into 2015.

sections.speaker: David Adlington
sections.content: I'm just going to try and push a little bit more on the 2017 guidance, and just whether you could give us any sort of help in terms of your margin assumptions, either by divisions, have preferably or maybe overall, going on that time frame, please?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: David, we have taken this really from a growth perspective. And therefore, we very deliberately, consciously wanted to refrain at this point to provide updates on individual business segment midterm outlooks. As I mentioned for Fresenius Kabi, bear with us. Also, it doesn't make a whole lot of sense for Fresenius Helios to put out a new guidance ahead of us being the proper owners of Helios. Finally, also Fresenius Medical Care, with its Capital Market Day coming up, we clearly didn't want to steal their thunder. So I'm afraid, David, you got to bear with us. But on the other hand, what you should work on as an assumption is that we will continue to see at least margin stability in our businesses going forward.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content: Okay, Jerry, if that's the case, thanks for joining us today.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Thanks for joining. We hope to -- we hope we were able to give you some more clarity on our expectations, and then, we'll talk to you again in Q1.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Thank you.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content:
Good afternoon in Europe. Good morning, in the U.S. Welcome to our first quarter earnings conference call and webcast. Thank you very much. Joining us today with us is Mark Schneider, CEO of Fresenius; and Stephan Sturm, the CFO. 
Before we get started, I'd like to mention that today's call is being recorded, and I'd also like to point you to the forward-looking statement disclosure and disclaimer wording on Page 2 of the presentation. And this cautionary language also applies to the presentation and comments to be made today. We hope that you had a chance to take a look at the presentation on our website. Mark Schneider will now start with a general overview and Stephan Sturm will lead you through the financials. And now I would like to hand it over to Mark. Mark, please?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content:
Thank you, Birgit, and a warm welcome to our conference call participants today. We appreciate your interest in our company. As planned, we have had a somewhat slow start into the year but can fully confirm our full year expectations for the group and all business segments. In addition, Fresenius Kabi's development gives us sufficient confidence to narrow our guidance for this business segment for the year and to be less conservative regarding the lower end of sales and margin expectations. Birgit has pointed out the safe harbor language to you so let's move right to Page 3, which summarizes our financial results for the quarter. 
Sales growth was strong and in particular, reflected the first time consolidation of a large part of the acquired Rhön hospitals as of January 1, 2014. As Stephan will describe later, we saw a significant currency headwinds due to euro strength. As a result, constant currency growth is 4% above actual currency growth in sales. EBIT growth is negative in both constant and actual currencies. This reflects negative EBIT developments at Fresenius Medical Care and Fresenius Kabi, partially offset by strong EBIT growth at Fresenius Helios and Fresenius Vamed. With lower interest expenses and tax payments, we nonetheless came to 3% constant currency net income growth, marking our 41st consecutive quarter of year-over-year net income growth. 
Page 4 provides more detail on the financial results by business segment. Please note that these are actual currency numbers. Constant currency sales growth for Fresenius Medical Care was 4% and for Fresenius Kabi, 1%. Currency also impacted EBIT growth at Fresenius Kabi. On the positive side, Fresenius Helios clearly stands out with the newly acquired hospitals delivering sales and EBIT fully in line with our expectations. Fresenius Vamed also had a good start to the year, in particular with regards to order entry. 
Before turning to Fresenius Kabi, HELIOs and Vamed in more detail, the brief word on Fresenius Medical Care. Please note that the company suffered in particular from combined year-over-year effects, such as U.S. sequestration and the 2013 April price increase. As outlined at the Capital Market Day in early April, the company will address near-term profitability issues with a global efficiency program and has exciting mid- to long-term expansion plans. Those steps will keep Fresenius Medical Care on a very attractive growth path. 
Turning to Page 5 now and Fresenius Kabi. Kabi also has to deal with several unfavorable year-over-year issues in the first quarter 2014. The most significant was certainly reduced sales for our blood volume expander HES. Which saw a EUR 20 million year-over-year sales decline. We did see the expected stabilization of the negative sales development with a sequential sales decline over Q4 2013 of only EUR 4 million. Sales in China for HES were particularly impacted by our decision from late last summer to discontinue the HES 200 product codes, which were a strong seller in that market. The negative growth effect will be behind us in the second half. 
Other issues, such as our change in the Russian distribution model and delayed tenders in Australia and Vietnam, are truly onetime in nature. We are therefore in a position to fully confirm our 2% to 5% organic sales growth target for Europe and our greater than 10% organic sales growth target for Asia-Pacific in 2014. So while Q1 was a particularly weak quarter in those areas, we expect to see a strong recovery, in particular, during the second half of the year. 
Let's turn to Page 6 and the North American market. Sales were essentially flat compared to Q1 2013, which was a meaningful accomplishment in light of the fact that we are seeing continued easing of the drug shortages in this market. In Q1 2013, there were 98 IV drugs on the FDA drug shortage list with 28 marketed by Fresenius Kabi North America. In April 2014, there were 68 IV drugs on the FDA drug shortage list, with 22 marketed by us. So while drug shortages are slow to ease, there is a noticeable improvement in the market which benefits the health care community and validates the combined efforts of FDA and industry to address the shortage situation. We had previously guided you to expect North American organic sales growth between minus 5% and plus 5% for this year. As a result of a somewhat better-than-expected first-quarter, we now believe that the range is going to be minus 2% to plus 5%. 
I would also like to give you an update on the U.S. shortage situation for sodium chloride, which I mentioned in our February conference call. We had no sodium chloride sales in the U.S. in Q1 2014, as it took us some time to prepare and ship the product. We recorded first commercial sales in the second half of April and FDA has recently granted an extension for the importation of 4.9% sodium chloride solution through the end of 2014. Please note that FDA may reconsider this discretion if the shortage risk is alleviated. At this point, we assume a double-digit million revenue opportunity in North America, which is part of our guidance for Kabi for the year 2014. When it comes to profitability, please note that in the interest of patient safety, we are also facing significantly higher costs. This includes FDA requirements on additional inspection, testing and repackaging. Freight costs for these products are also significantly higher. As a result, you should not expect an upside to Kabi's North American operating margin. Also note that our available capacity is limited. As much as we would like to support the U.S. health care community at this critical time, we are also mindful of our obligations to the health care community in Europe. 
Let's now turn to HELIOs. HELIOs has seen a successful quarter, both for its established hospitals and the newly acquired Rhön locations. There were no integration cost in Q1 and so the transaction was already accretive to earnings in that quarter. Integration continues to proceed very well and we have found no major surprises regarding the performance of the acquired hospital locations or the integration process as such. One significant Rhön location has not closed yet and that is Rhön-Klinikum in the city of Wiesbaden. We expect closing at the latest by the end of June this year. We would therefore like to wait till our Q2 conference call to give you full year 2014 HELIOs guidance, including all newly acquired Rhön facilities. In the interest of precise and reliable expectations, this patience should pay off. Until then, please accept our assurances that we were very pleased with the results of the locations we have acquired so far. 
Let's turn briefly to Vamed on Page 7. Vamed had a good start to the year and enjoyed particularly strong order entry from emerging markets. At this point, we are fully confirming our sales and EBIT guidance for Vamed for the year 2014. Before handing over to Stephan, a final comment on strategic initiatives for Fresenius Kabi. As you know, Kabi generates almost 1/3 of its revenues in high-growth emerging markets. With selective acquisitions and joint ventures, we have expanded our emerging market presence during the last year and we have a promising transaction pipeline. The ongoing emerging market currency weakness should not detract from the fact that these country markets will see continued expansion in the health care systems, which would drive demand for our products. 
While it is our policy not to comment on all specific transactions that are rumored, let me also confirm that all M&A projects that Kabi is currently working on are small to midsize in nature. We are not pursuing any large transactions in the billion-euro-plus category at the present time. 
I think all of us have noticed a very active rumor mill regarding M&A in recent weeks, in particular in the pharma sector. It was therefore important to me to assure you that we were not participating in this megadeal frenzy. Our primary focus at this stage is on operations and only small- to mid-scale M&A. With this, I would like to hand it over to Stephan. Thank you and talk to you later.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content:
Thank you, Mark. Good afternoon, and good morning, warm welcome to everybody. You heard it from Mark, not an overwhelming start into 2014 but that was to be expected. As usual, let's start with the group P&L and that's on Slide 9. Sales came in at just above EUR 5.2 billion, that is 11% growth at constant currency, so a bit shy of our full year guidance of 12% to 15%, but please bear in mind that some of the newly acquired Rhön hospitals will consolidate only late in the quarter and that Rhön-Klinikum clinic in Wiesbaden would only be considered later this year. Plus, we expect Kabi organic growth to pick up, but I'll come to that later. 
Reported growth was 7%, so with minus 4 points. We saw yet again of very meaningful currency impact. U.S. dollars softness is the single largest factor here, but weakness of Latin America and Asian currencies combined has about the same effect of the group. Acquisitions contributed almost 9% in the first quarter, that's largely the newly acquired Rhön hospitals. Organic growth was about 2.5%, softer than alike, but the first quarter of last year is a tough comp that we're up against and arguably the toughest comp all year. EBIT reached EUR 643 million and that's down 8% at constant currency. FMC will explain the impacts -- the effects impacting them on their call. Outside FMC, EBIT was flat year-on-year at constant currency. So HELIOs and Vamed nicely offset the footfall at Kabi. Net interest was EUR 138 million in first quarter, including that [ph]. So we were slightly below the run rate I had guided to, x the Rhön financing. But you will have seen that the long-term financing for the acquisition was only put in place over the course of the quarter. Until then, we were able to rely on cheaper short-term instruments. So Rhön financing expenses will be approximately EUR 80 million this year and EUR 90 million next. 
I'd like to confirm our underlying run rate, x Rhön, of EUR 135 million per quarter and therefore also our full year guidance of EUR 610 million to EUR 630 million all-in. And that's a very solid source of earnings growth this year. 
The tax rate in the group improved to 26.3% in Q1 from 27.8% for the full year of '13. Key reason is a onetime effect at FMC that Mike would elaborate on later. This was foreseen and therefore, I would like to confirm our 29% guidance for the full year. The somewhat higher implied tax rate for the rest of the year should not be taken as a proxy for 2015 and beyond. Geographical mix of earnings before tax is a key driver of the group tax rate and going forward, we believe in the growing contribution from lower tax rate regions. 
Finally, net income. That came in at EUR 228 million, up 2% reported and up 3% at constant currency, so already well within our guidance range. And for the group, excluding the FMC, earnings growth was above 6%. 
Finally, as a housekeeping item, you know we had to dispose of 2 HELIOs clinics in order to make the Rhön transaction happen. We are showing the resulting EUR 21 million book gain as an extraordinary item below the line. I expect this amount to be eaten up over the course of the year by Rhön and further Fenwal integration expenses. 
With that, let's move over to Kabi on Slide 10.  There, EBIT reached EUR 201 million, down 15% year-on-year, including a 2% point hit from currency translation. Now clearly, also a 13% decrease is not what we're used to and we hate it, but most of the drivers are well known. We did talk about HES, overall, a double-digit million euro item in this past quarter. We did talk about China, a separate, single-digit-million euro item, key issues that kicked in either in late Q1 or in Q2 last year and therefore, we'll see the anniversary later this year. 
Also, do bear in mind that in the first quarter of last year, we were still sole supplier of Propofol in the U.S. So for all of these issues, the comps will moderate from here. But importantly, we also had a few true onetimers that were weighing on Kabi's performance and where we therefore believe in an underlying improvement in the coming quarters. 
Onto the regions where Europe bore the brunt of the overall HES impact. The margin decline was aggravated by a change in our distribution model in Russia, ahead of forming the joint venture with Rhön-Klinikum. That is a single-digit million euro item that won't recur. So we firmly believe in the margin pickup going forward. My earlier guidance, though looking for full year margin in line with that of full year '13, that has been too ambitious. 
In North America, we were able to maintain our margin from last year's first quarter despite a lower Propofol market share. Given that we continue to believe in the fairly rapid normalization of the drug shortage situation, I'd like to confirm our earlier guidance of the full year margin in the low- to mid-30s. Within that range, however, we have turned more optimistic. Low 30s actually would be a surprise. 
In the emerging markets, we saw the currency mix effect I was referring to as part of our full year presentation. In China, in particular, we saw part of the overall HES effect, plus the anniversary of last year's price cuts. And over and above the typical slow start to just any year, we saw the late tenders in Australia and Vietnam. That's another single-digit-million euro item. So just as in Europe, we expect the growth and margin pickup going forward. Finally for corporate and R&D, we believe in a somewhat lower run rate than the EUR 64 million we're showing for the first quarter. 
Over to HELIOs on Slide 11, where sales and EBIT growth was clearly driven by first-time consolidation of the hospitals we acquired from  Rhön. Stripping those out, we saw very decent organic sales growth of 4%, right in the middle of our guidance range. We're still not entirely happy with organic patient growth, but by now, we have identified a small number of different nonrelated factors that have held us back, some of them very hospital-specific. In our minds, none of them is longer-lasting and we have already developed appropriate accounting initiatives. 
Total EBIT was EUR 114 million with the newly acquired hospitals diluting the margin by about 1 point. In the established clinic portfolio, at HELIOs we saw EBIT of EUR 88 million at a stable 10.3 -- 10.3% margin. Please bear in mind that the 2 hospitals we had to divest were very nicely profitable. So against that backdrop, we're fine with that result. The newly acquired hospitals contributed an EBIT of EUR 26 million in Q1 with a margin of 7%. Also that is very much in line with our expectations. No major surprises so far and the risk of finding one is shrinking by the day. And notwithstanding the very much expected margin dilution, the former Rhön hospitals have been earnings accretive right from the start and also would have been earnings accretive against the normalized run rate for the financing expenses. 
Let's move on to cash flow on Slide 12 where operating cash flow was light in Q1, well below last year, unavoidable [ph] because the 2 key reasons driving this development were both onetime in nature: A, FMC making the great settlement payment eventually, as we should say, as this was meant to happen for about the last 10 years; B, Vamed this time not receiving the typical EUR 100 million-plus prepayment for services to be rendered over the course of the year. That customer will now pay in monthly installments, and that's okay. At Kabi, we saw DSOs in North America slightly capping out. I'm not worried about that at all. Credit quality there is not an issue, and that effect will normalize in the coming quarters, I'm sure. But rather, I was glad to see a slight reduction of HELIOs' DSOs and we'll keep working on that. 
Let's conclude with an update on our guidance on Slides 13 and therefore the group on 14. First though on our business segment, excluding FMC. As discussed, for Kabi's sales growth, we expect a healthful combination of underlying pickup and easing comps, so we will grow in to that range. Whilst we confirm our 2% to 5% guidance for Europe, we're gradually more conservative, hence we're cutting the top end of Kabi's guidance by 1 point. But likewise, we are gradually more optimistic in North America. Hence, we're lifting the bottom end. And pretty much the same is true for Kabi's margin. With 16.6% in Q1, we are in the original guidance range already. And given our expectations for the individual regions that I explained, we're comfortable lifting the bottom end. 
Now for HELIOs, organic growth in Q1 is already well within our guidance range. As discussed, I'm slightly optimistic that patient growth will pick up going forward, but it's way too early to tinker with our guidance. Though EBIT of the old HELIOs portfolio, if you simply took the EUR 88 million from Q1 as run rate, we'd be shy of the EUR 390 million to EUR 410 million range we're guiding to. But you know, that we have traditionally grown that rate over the course of the year. This year will be no different. From experience, the percentage contribution of Q1 to full year EBIT in the low 20s and therefore, we're very comfortable with that the EUR 390 million to EUR 410 million range. 
For the Rhön portfolio as Mark explained, given the early stage of integration and the varying consolidation dates, Q1 is not fully representative for the remainder of 2014. Again, we're happy with what we've seen so far, but our Q2 call will be a much better occasion for providing you with a reliable guidance. 
For Vamed, the minus 2% organic growth in the first quarter, that reflects the typical volatility we experience in Vamed's project business. We take a lot of comfort from Vamed's strong order book and their very predictable service business. So we continue to be very comfortable with our 5% to 10% guidance range for the top line, and we have no reason to believe that EBIT shouldn't follow. 
So for the group on Slide 14, we have seen 11% constant currency growth for our top line in the first quarter, but as discussed, we will experience easing comps plus in particular, at Kabi, an underlying pickup, potentially at HELIOs, most certainly at Vamed. In addition, we will see the first-time consolidation of Rhön-Klinikum in Wiesbaden. I'm very comfortable with that 12% to 15% range. Likewise for earnings, where we are already at 3% after the first quarter, and given all the developments that I described, I see no reason whatsoever to amend that guidance of 2% to 5%. 
Finally, I know that many of you were somewhat surprised about the magnitude of the foreign exchange translation effect in Q1. For the top line, we were 4 points below constant currency growth and given where we are right now, I'd also expect 3 to 4 points for the full year. For earnings after tax, the transition effect was minus 1 point in the first quarter. And where I'm sitting right now, I'd expect 1 to 2 points for the full year. 
I hope that was helpful so far. Mark and I will now be happy to take your questions. Thank you for now.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: First of all on Fenwal. Stephan, you have in the past kindly given us the breakout of Fenwal's EBIT. I was wondering if you could give us that for first quarter. And I'm sorry if I'd missed it. And also, while on the Fenwal, I understand your caution on the overall Kabi guidance, but one thing we haven't mentioned today is the American Red Cross contract, which, given the commentary from your competitor, would suggest that you've won that. Could you talk to the materiality of that contract win?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Ed, thanks for your question. Stephan -- I'll kick it off and Mark's going to comment on the American Red Cross. You hadn't missed it because I have no intention of providing a separate Fenwal number. And there is no bad will from me, it is just as you would expect. The business in the meantime is integrated into Kabi to a degree, that it is virtually impossible to strip out a separate number for the Fenwal business. So again, no bad will, no lack of transparency, but much rather a sign of progressing integration into Kabi.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: And Ed, let me build on that. As you know, the business plan from the beginning was to really put this together with our own transfusion preexisting business and make a combined entity that really can be a world-class supplier, and this is what we're doing. And I think for the first few quarters, it was different, but at some point, it really becomes part of the Kabi business mix. On the American Red Cross tender, I can't confirm we've won it. We're highly appreciative of the opportunity to work with the American Red Cross. But just like last time when the outcome was still very much in doubt, we would still like to stay away from commenting on individual tenders. I don't think that's a prudent policy to do and therefore, this is part of our normal business going forward. Again, we highly welcome on the opportunity to work with ARC, but over and above that, I wouldn't want to comment.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: I understand. I absolutely understand that, but would you say the level of materiality of the contract guided to you by your competitor was in the right ballpark?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: At the risk of being not helpful, but I'd like to comment even less on our competitors. I think for us, it is what it is, and there's nothing more to say about it. Sorry.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: Okay. As a quick follow on, we just...

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Ed, let me just say, there seems to be a common misperception in the market that we're not happy with Fenwal's performance so that the business is underperforming. We'd like to strongly dispute that. We are absolutely happy with what we're seeing right now and what we have seen in the past. Sorry for interrupting you.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: No, absolutely. And clearly, the level of that contract win would suggest that you certainly are performing more strongly. Just a quick housekeeping item, just in terms of the phasing of the integration cost of Fenwal for this year?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Ed, we've seen a slow start in that respect. We stick to the overall guidance of that we have provided, but it would be more backloaded in 2014.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: I have 3, if I can. The first one was I was just hoping we could get an update on Grand Island and whether you have any further insights into when we might be seeing a reinspection from the FDA? My second question is just around the Kabi margins in Europe and Asia-Pacific. And Stephan, thank you for giving us the disclosure around some of the sort of transient issues that impacted profitability in Q1. Though I have to say, even when I adjust for those, it does seem like the business year-on-year is seeing further margin compression. And I guess, what would be helpful to understand is just what of that is coming from the annualization of HES in China and what of that is actually underlying? And as you think about the margin for these businesses on a regional basis into '15 and beyond, how we should be thinking about it? And the last one was just Mark, on your comment on M&A, can you clarify by what you had in mind by small and midsize? We've all been wondering very much about a certain nutritional business and I don't expect you to comment on that, but it sort of -- whether it fits into the mold of what potential transactions you're looking at? That will be very helpful.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: And let me take questions 1 and 3 and then hand you to Stephan for the margin question. On Grand Island, we've put in a lot of work and feel really good about it. We have not had any indication from FDA when we might get re-audited. It is our expectation that this will occur sometime this year, but that expectation is solely built on our past experience, like, well, the time interval between audits, which is typically 1 to 2 years. And as you know, the last audit occurred in December 2012, January 2013, so this would suggest that sometime later this year we will see another audit. And I feel very good about the work that has been done and about the attention this has been getting from Kabi top management, which is fully committed to meeting FDA's expectations. Regarding M&A, I was trying to follow sort of the established pattern we had for deal sizes. So a small transaction for us would be double-digit million euros, so EUR 10 million, EUR 20 million, EUR 30 million, sometimes even below the materiality threshold, something we wouldn't even disclose because it's so minor. Midsize for us starts at about EUR 100 million and would extend to several hundred million euros. And good examples would be one large maximum care hospital or typically sort of a larger distribution or larger manufacturing at the Kabi space in one particular country. And then, large would be a billion euros-plus. So that sort of the size buckets that we've worked with in the past. And so, all I was trying to say is our main focus is on integration and operations at this time. There is always, as you know, some background activity in small to midsize that's ongoing. That could be for example, individual FMC dialysis clinics, smaller Kabi operations or individual hospitals. And that's essentially what's going on now. But we're not working on that large bucket of EUR 1 billion-plus.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: And Veronika, at the risk of not being truly helpful, I was really trying to be fairly transparent as part of my prepared comments already. And we have those transient items that hit particularly Europe and Asia-Pacific, first and foremost HES. And there, we're really talking about a stabilization but not the expectation that this revenue and EBIT is going to come back. And there, as Mark has described, this is an item that is well into the double digits in this past Q1. On the other hand, we have the China price cut where we do not expect a near-term recovery. But on the other hand, as explained on previous occasions, in contrast to what we see as a gradual price decrease, price erosion in many other countries, the experience in China says that we have few, but that fewer, but therefore more market steps. And that in between those price cuts steps, we always, given very attractive volume growth, have the chance, given economies of scale, to recover part of the initial -- at least, part of the original margin loss. Over and above that, we have something that in our minds is truly onetime in nature and is really something that falls on not just on the side of Q1, but potentially, hopefully, on the side of this coming or current second quarter. With regard to your question about the medium-term margins, we owe you a more formal Kabi margin guidance for the medium term. But frankly, as described on earlier occasions, the top end of the 18% to 21% margin guidance that formally is still out there was never reached and, therefore, still going forward, should be viewed as being overly optimistic. On the other hand, what we need to do is that we need to adjust for a higher contribution of the very attractive, but margin dilutive medical devices business. Take all that together and from where we are right now, I wouldn't expect a meaningful change from that underlying framework.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: And if I can just quick follow-up, Stephan. Can you give us a sense in terms of APP, what the pricing contributions of growth was in the quarter?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: No general trends, Veronika, and they're very much varying across different products.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: I've got a couple of, hopefully, fairly easy questions to answer. Just following on from Veronika's question, I was just wondering if you could very briefly tell us where we are with your other 2 warning letters, the one in Kalyani and the one there in Puerto Rico. The second question is just on the hospital business. I wondered if you could just very briefly comment on what you're doing to improve the volumes in facilities that were affected. And perhaps, answer the question within the context of whether you felt there was in the operational disruption in Q1 from the integration of Rhön. I know it's very hard to quantify, but just maybe some qualitative comment on whether you felt your performance and the legacy HELIOs businesses would've been slightly better had you not been trying to integrate all the Rhön facilities. And then the third question was just one that [indiscernible] your decision to discontinue HES in China. Given that it was such a reasonable size product in the Asia-Pac region for you, what drove that decision? Was that your decision for some reason? Or was that forced upon you by external factors?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes. This is Mark. Let me comment on the warning letters first. On Kalyani, we are basically in the process of restarting that facility. These products have a pretty significant manufacturing and lead time, but we're gearing up for shipments later this quarter, and that includes shipments to the U.S. where we've had interaction with the FDA. And for Kalyani, just like with Grand Island, we're expecting another audit by FDA. Based on past experience, I would think that this would happen this year. Regarding Puerto Rico, this is the one that we had mentioned in our slide deck, our business Maricao, and there I have the good use that in pretty quick time, the warning letter got lifted and so we are operating here with perfect status at that plant. Regarding the hospital business, I don't think there has been significant disruption when it comes to operations from the integration process. First of all, this is something we couldn't compromise on. Operations and patients always basically take top priority. So if in doubt, an equation has to move slower. And also keep in mind, these are facilities that have gone through an integration process already at some point in time in the past when they were privatized from the government and purchased by Rhön. So I think compared to that, the change process now is much less significant and people are used to it. So overall, I think the integration process has started as well as it only could. And I don't foresee any major operational disruption that would be meaningful for the entire company, HELIOs. Regarding HES 200, this is not the entire HES product range. This is simply just one particular molecular weight, the 200 dalton molecular weight. And that was a voluntary global process that we kicked off in August-September last year as part of some of the findings we've had. And so, all I was  trying to say is that this product in particular did sell well in China, but when we have insights and medical discussions in Europe, we don't make a difference whether it's in Europe or somewhere else. We just felt that's the right thing to do, and so we discontinued shipping their product.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Do you think you'll get some of their sales back on other formulations of it? Or are they pretty much gone forever you think?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: I think there will be some leakage into other formulations, but overall, keep in mind that this particular product group right now is just about stabilizing. We're not talking about new areas of growth. And hence, I think for now, it's safer to assume that there's no significant flowback.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
sections.content: I have just a few quick questions. I mean on remediation growth [ph], did you have any expense in the quarter by chance?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Chris, we had, as part of our full year call, said that we stick to our overall guidance of up to EUR 100 million. We had told you that in 2013, EUR 80 million out of that was spent. And yes, part of that residual was also accrued in the first quarter of 2014.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
sections.content: Okay. But the maturity of that? Or in all, basically, just sure to be -- just think about it evenly spread over the rest of the years or kind of..

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Part of it was spent in Q1.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
