sections.content: Okay, great. And then the second question is on Propofol in the U.S., it looks like now your market share holding up really well. I was wondering if there any difference in all in terms of profitability, whether you sell Propofol or Diprivan?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: We haven't commented on that in the past, but the 2 products are pretty close to each other. So I think from a total materiality point of view, there's not much difference.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
sections.content: Okay. And then, the third question is could you maybe talk about the growth trends of IV generic drugs outside of the U.S.?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Those are pretty much in place, and as you know, this is something that's always driven by our registration and approval activity, market launches and also in terms of products [indiscernible] it's driven by product availability. But overall, yes, I mean, most of our discussions with you focus on the U.S. because IMS data makes it so easy to track that. But as we pointed out in previous calls, there's a significant size of IV drug business outside the U.S. as well, and that also, just like in the U.S., is one of our fastest growing product groups and does really well. But there is much harder to talk about aggregate data because it's a country-by-country situation that we're looking at.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
sections.content: Okay. But you don't see any change in growth trends? Because if I do my math, I get to a bit of low number in Q1. But it could also be that I have the wrong assumptions.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes, not sure about the math. But no, we have no reason to be particularly concerned about it.

sections.speaker: Christoph Gretler
sections.content: Okay. And then maybe the last question, I mean, I fully respect that you don't want to talk about the integration at -- from the Rhön-Klinikum hospitals into HELIOs and the margin impact, but basically, we obviously have to do some models here. So if you think about this 100 basis point dilution impact you have seen Q1 and as a bit, as you know, we have now the full effect than in Q2 and onwards. Would that be a reasonable base now?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Chris, I believe we pointed you to a starting point of the Rhön portfolio of approximately 7.5% EBIT margin, and that is a full year margin and that seasonality that I was talking about for the HELIOs portfolio, obviously, also applies for that subset. That is part of the reason why I'm saying very much in line with expectations, happy with what we're seeing. Also, do bear in mind, I alluded to that, that not the entire portfolio was with us for the entire first quarter. And therefore, the EUR 26 million is not a representative starting point, not even for what you would model in terms of a quarterly pickup.

sections.speaker: Julien Dormois
sections.content: I'm left with 2 questions. The first one relates to the sales guidance for Kabi and the refined sales guidance. So if understand correctly, you have slightly raised the bottom end of the range because of the U.S., and as to show you -- the shortage situation. But I'm just stating to reconcile why you have cut the upper end from 7% to 6% because I think Europe, U.S. and rest of the world remain pretty much where they were after the full-year. So I was just trying to understand. And the second one is basically housekeeping question regarding the integration cost for Rhön. So if I understand, you have not incurred any cost in Q1. I was just wondering if your guidance for EUR 65 million for the full year still stands.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: So let me start with the first question, then hand it to Stephan for the integration costs. You're quite right, we also took one percentage point off the top, and when you think back to that pretty wide 4 percentage point band, 3% to 7% that we talked about in February, clearly, we did not know where exactly the drug shortage situation in the U.S. was going. It has been probably a stronger quarter in the U.S. than we expected, but we also have not seen, for example, a worsening of the drug shortage. And hence, there was no particular reason for expecting the very high end of that range. And then also, since we discussed Europe and Asia-Pacific in detail, as Stephan outlined, a lot of this was expected, but there were also a few items, even though they're onetime, they were unexpected and were not expected in that magnitude. And hence, both areas performed a little weaker than we thought and so, given both of these efforts, the fact that in the U.S., drug shortage has not turned worse and that Europe and Asia-Pacific have performed a little less well than we thought, I think there's no reason to really maintain that top percentage [indiscernible] 6% at 7%. So all we wanted to do is to be as precise and helpful to you as we can, given what we know today.

sections.speaker: Julien Dormois
sections.content: Julien, it's Stephan. Integration costs, what you've got to understand is that we really set only foot in the door at the end of February or in early March. So you're right, no integration cost in this past first quarter. But also that was not to be expected just after 1 month of being the owners. It is too early to fully reconfirm in particular a breakdown of the overall aggregate amount of EUR 65 million between '14 and '15. There will no doubt be integration cost in the coming quarters, I alluded to that. At this point, I also have no integration that the number is going to be meaningfully different from the EUR 65 million that we indicated earlier. But also here, please bear with us until we become more specific and more reliable on July 31.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content: Okay, thanks, Christine. And thanks to everybody joining the call today. We appreciate your time and your questions.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Thanks, and talk to you second quarter.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Thank you. Bye-bye.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content:
Thanks, Christine. Good afternoon in Europe. Good morning in the U.S. Welcome to our Second Quarter Earnings Conference Call and Webcast. Thank you very much to everyone joining the call. I'm very pleased to introduce to you Mark Schneider, CEO; and Stephan Sturm, CFO. 
Let me say that today's call is being recorded, and I'd like to draw your attention to the forward-looking statement and disclaimer wording in the presentation on Page 2. This cautionary language also applies to the presentation and comments to be made today. 
Mark Schneider will now provide you with a general overview, and Stephan Sturm will take you through the financials. Afterwards, management is prepared to answer your questions. 
Now I would like to hand it over to Mark. Mark, please?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content:
Thank you, Birgit, and a warm welcome to our conference call participants today. As always, we appreciate your interest in our company. We are pleased to report solid progress in our financial results across the board in the second quarter and acceleration of growth after the expected slow start into the year. All of our guidance components are either fully confirmed or slightly improved. The integration of the newly acquired Rhön hospitals is proceeding very well, and we are finding the expected earnings upsides. This gets reflected in our newly HELIOS full year EBIT guidance, which includes the acquired Rhön facilities for the first time. 
Birgit has pointed out the Safe Harbor language to you, so let's move right to Page 3, which summarizes our financial results for the quarter.  
Constant currency sales growth was strong and meaningfully above Q1 levels. We have seen the benefits of revised organic growth, and of course, the first time consolidation of the acquired Rhön hospitals. EBIT development is still slightly negative at actual currency rates, but flat at constant currency rates, which marks a significant improvement from Q1. With lower interest expenses and tax payments, we came to 3% constant currency net income growth, marking our 42nd consecutive quarter of year-over-year net income growth. 
Page 4 provides more detail on the financial results by business segment. With the exception of Vamed where the project business is more backloaded this year than in 2013, we're seeing across-the-board improvements in sales and EBIT growth rates compared to Q1. Looking at a development of Vamed's order entry levels and their all-time high order backlog, we have every reason to expect a very strong second half for this business segment. 
Before turning to Fresenius Kabi, HELIOS and Vamed in more detail, a brief word on Fresenius Medical Care. We saw improving fundamentals in the second quarter and are also getting traction on both near-term and midterm savings as part of the global efficiency program. In addition to that, the company made good progress on its strategic objectives, in particular, building out a stronger presence in North American patient care coordination activities. So all in all, we're very pleased with progress in the second quarter. 
Turning to Page 5 now and Fresenius Kabi. In North America, we saw a slight uptick in organic sales growth to 1% in the second quarter, even though the drug shortage situation is easing. As of last week, there were 64 drugs on the drug shortage list with 21 being marketed by Fresenius Kabi. For the full year 2014, we expect North American sales to be roughly flat but a lot will depend on how exactly the drug shortage situation develops. Sales of imported Kabi saline solutions commenced in the second quarter and amounted to about EUR 3 million for the quarter. 
While covering North America, let me also give you an update on quality and FDA matters. We have proactively requested an inspection of our Grand Island facility by FDA. At this point, we do not have any information from the agency on possible dates. We recognize that the combined team at FDA is pretty busy right now. Any inspection will be unannounced as is normal with FDA compliance orders. You should see our inspection request as a sign of our quality commitment and confidence in the work that was accomplished on site. 
I know there are some concerns regarding the resolution of the Grand Island warning letter and potential impacts for 2015 sales growth in North America. Let me reassure you that the Grand Island warning letter will not restrict our major launches for 2015. And that even if the warning letter is not lifted, we will -- we still expect our launches to be at the upper end of our normal guidance of 6 to 10 products per year. For the current year, we have received 2 approvals so far, and as expected at the beginning of the year, we will probably come in slightly below our normal annual run rate of 6 to 10 products. 
While we're on the subject of FDA matters, let's turn to our facility in Kalyani, India. We have restarted production. Shipments to destinations outside of the U.S. have commenced in Q2. We expect shipments to the U.S. later this quarter. Since we received the Kalyani warning letter of last year, FDA has not inspected the site. Based on past inspection intervals, we expect an FDA audit at the site at any time. 
Earlier this year, the U.K.'s MHRA inspected the site successfully and confirmed GMP compliance. 
Back to the U.S. market. Our North American Propofol market share, as per IMS, has decreased by more than 10% since Q1. In case you missed this, please note that this decrease is overstated due to the inclusion of certain direct sales by IMS since April. Unlike our competitors, we do not report our direct sales to IMS. About 7% to 8% of the market share reduction is attributable to this IMS change. The balance, only about 3% to 5% of market share, was lost due to increasing shipments by our competitors. 
In Europe, Asia Pacific and Latin America, we saw revised organic growth. Please note the strong European organic sales growth development, excluding our blood volume extender, HES, in the second quarter. HES sales have stabilized in Q2 compared to Q1 and we have reason to believe that the period of sequential HES sales declines is over. The year-over-year decrease in the second quarter for this product was EUR 18 million, 1-8. 
Let's now turn to HELIOS. HELIOS has seen another very successful quarter, both for its established hospitals and the newly acquired Rhön locations. We are in the middle of  a massive integration job and are really pleased with how well it is going. Margin upsides that we expected before the transaction are clearly there and our integration process is fully on track. At this point, we can confirm our annual cost synergy target of EUR 85 million by 2015 with a caveat that there's a 1 quarter time delay due to the delayed closing of the acquisition. We can also confirm expected integration costs of EUR 80 million. Phase-in detail on the integration costs between 2014 and 2015 will follow in our Q3 conference call. 
As Stephan will explain later, our new combined 2014 EBIT guidance for the HELIOS and Rhön facilities makes it very clear that EBIT margin liftoff at the acquired facilities has already begun this year. It is important to us to be reliable stewards of your capital and making good on our acquisition projections for such a massive deal is at the center of our attention. 
Due to a revised antitrust analysis, we have also been granted commission to acquire Rhön's Cuxhaven hospital, which will add another EUR 40 million of annual sales as from August 1, 2014. 
Let me also point out that we're making good progress on the hospital network between HELIOS, Rhön-Klinikum and Asklepios. There is a strong interest from other hospital operators to be part of this. We expect to get the necessary approvals and contracts put in place this quarter and launch the network and marketing activities in the fourth quarter of this year.  
Admissions development in our HELIOS facilities, which was an area of concern in Q1, improved in Q2 and reached 1.1%. As we expected, the various issues leading to negative Q1 admissions development are mostly behind us and we expect continued positive admissions development in the second half of 2014. 
On this note, let me hand it over to Stephan, and I will be happy to answer your questions later.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content:
Thank you, Mark. Good afternoon, good morning. A warm welcome to everybody. You heard it from Mark. After the expected slow start in Q1, we saw a decent acceleration of growth in the second quarter and so  I'm reasonably happy with that quarter. 
Let's go to the group P&L, and that's on Slide 8. Sales came in just above EUR 5.5 billion in the second quarter to reach EUR 10.7 billion in the first half. That is a 13% growth at constant currency for Q2, and given the 11% in Q1, 12% for the first half. Despite an even stronger negative currency effect in Q2, that was 5 points versus 4 points in Q1, reported growth increased to 8% in Q2, leading to 7% in the first half, driven by almost all segments. 
The foreign exchange headwind stems from the very same currencies as in Q1. U.S. dollar softness is the single largest factor, combined with the weakness of some LATAM and Asian currencies. 
However, as of now, I do expect a mitigated foreign exchange effect for the rest of 2014 given dollar weakness in the second half of last year. 
Now within the 13% constant currency growth, acquisitions contributed 10% in the second quarter. That is up from 9% in Q1. Largest driver remained the newly acquired Rhön hospitals and they are also the key reason for that slight acceleration in Q2. As you know, a few hospitals were only consolidated as of March 1 and those have now contributed for the full quarter. We expect further increased growth contribution from acquisitions in the second half as we will take on HSK hospital in Wiesbaden and FMC's announced acquisitions from Q3. 13% at constant currency minus 10% from acquisitions, so we're looking at a slightly increased organic growth of just above 3% in the second quarter, up from about 2.5% in Q1. The improvement was largely driven by FMC followed by Kabi. 
For the second half, we believe in a further pickup of organic growth pretty much across all segments, driven by both and underlying improvement, as well as by moderated comps. 
All in all, therefore, we expect a meaningful acceleration of reported growth in the second half for 3 reasons: number one, pickup of organic growth; number two, increasing contribution from acquisitions; number three, likely mitigation of the so far negative currency impact. 
On to EBIT and that reached EUR 760 million, up 5% in Q2 at constant currency, offsetting the negative growth in Q1 for flat first half. As always, FMC will explain their effects on their own call. But outside FMC, EBIT growth was even higher, 9% in the second quarter at constant currency, taking the first half to 5%. This was, again, largely driven by HELIOS, but Kabi also managed to significantly narrow the gap to last year's contribution. 
All business segments showed sequential margin improvement. 
And for the second half, we do expect further EBIT growth, taking the full year clearly above last year's level. 
Net interest was EUR 145 million in Q2, resulting in EUR 283 million for the first half. Now earlier this year, I was quoting a quarterly run rate of EUR 22 million, EUR 23 million for the Rhön financing and I'd like to confirm that now. Before our base financing, I was guiding to roughly EUR 135 million per quarter. So far, we've come in below that primarily because of decreasing market interest rates. 
For the second half, though, we will incur higher interest expenses, driven primarily by FMC's acquisitions. Therefore, I'd like to confirm a quarterly run rate of approximately EUR 135 million, excluding the Rhön financing for the second half. And taken all together, for the full year, that should take us to the bottom end of my previous guidance of EUR 610 million to EUR 630 million despite current dollar strength. 
The group tax rate was at an extraordinary 32.4% in this past Q2 and that took the first half to 29.6%, up more than a point year-on-year. Key reason is a onetime hit at FMC and Mike, I'm sure, will elaborate on that later. That came unexpected and is truly onetime in nature. So for the coming quarters, I'd like to stick to my guidance of approximately 29%. And consequentially, for the full year, we should be just slightly above that level. 
Finally, net income before integration costs and disposal gains, that was EUR 259 million in Q2, up 3% at constant currency. The same growth rate as in Q1, notwithstanding the FMC tax hit. 
And therefore, for the group, excluding FMC, we saw even higher earnings growth, 7% at constant currency, both in Q2 and the first half. And for the very avoidance of doubt, the net income we're showing here excludes the substantial disposal gains we have made, as well as the, so far, only minor integration costs, year-to-date a net positive amount of almost EUR 50 million. Those are reported below the line. 
With that, let's move over to Kabi on Slide 9. First, total EBIT at the bottom of the page where Q2 came in at EUR 210 million, reported a 9% decrease year-on-year. That is, however, including minus 4 points from currency translation. So at constant currency, Q2 EBIT is down only by 5%. That is a major improvement from the minus 13% in Q1. So Q2 saw a substantial catch-up and we managed to narrow the gap. This is due to both an underlying improvement and in easing of the year-on-year comps. 
In Q2, we were already pairing apples with apples as far as the China price cuts are concerned. From Q3, that will increasingly be the case for HES as well, and from Q4, also for last year's China anticorruption investigation. Given all that, we expect a further margin pickup in the second half. 
In Europe, top of the slide, that is most definitely the case. We already saw a margin pickup to 15.5% in Q2, up from 13.8%, and we do expect margins well beyond the Q2 level in the second half. 
In North America, the margin was basically flat quarter-on-quarter. Our base case proceeds margin erosion for the second half to the mid-30s, driven by further easing of drug shortages. This, by the way, implies a margin in the high 30s for the IV drug part of the North American business. 
In the emerging markets, we expect the pickup has actually materialized, in particular, in China. And also here, I do expect a further margin increase in the second half. 
Corporate R&D was EUR 69 million in the second quarter. That included a few unexpected items. I said in early May that the Q1 number was EUR 64 million and that was a good proxy. In essence, I stick to that. And so for the second half, I believe in a quarterly run rate is the low to mid 60s. 
In summary, we expect margin erosion in North America, which may or may not materialize; a margin pickup in Europe and the emerging markets; and lower absolute corporate and R&D expense relative to a growing top line. Taken all together, that should lead to margin expansion in the second half over the first. 
Now over to HELIOS on Slide 10 where sales increased to around EUR 1.3 billion in Q2, driven primarily by the newly acquired Rhön hospitals. Bear in mind that some of these were only consolidated as for March 1 and now contributed a full quarter. 
Coming back to our discussion from early May regarding organic patient growth. There, in Q2, we saw the turn we were hoping for. The various initiatives bore fruit and a decent 1.1% growth offset the negative number from the first quarter and we expect growth at around this level also for the coming quarters. 
Organic sales growth was rounded up 4% in the first quarter and 3% in Q2, so actually not that much of a difference and that, therefore, led to a strong 3% in the first half. 
Total EBIT at the bottom of the slide was EUR 136 million, nicely ahead of the EUR 114 million we were showing in Q1. And whilst we saw an almost 1 point margin progression in the established clinic portfolio, the lion's share of the absolute EBIT increase was, again, coming from the Rhön portfolio. 
There, the margin, after 7% in Q1, ticked up to 9.1%, taking the first half to 8.2%. 
You may recall our transaction announcement last September, we were saying that the acquired portfolio and annual sales of approximately EUR 2 billion and an EBITDA of roughly EUR 250 million. That is a margin of 12.5%, leading to an EBIT margin of approximately 7.5%. So it's good to see that we're already ahead of that. 
However, I want to caution you about using the 9.1% Q2 margin as a near-term proxy. Bear in mind that we'll consolidate Wiesbaden from Q3, and given that there, we expect the negative operating result, at least this year, our margin will be diluted. 
Let's move on to cash flow on Slide 11 and starting with the group at the bottom of the page. LTM operating cash flow margin was 10%. I'm very happy with that given the burdens of the Grace settlement payment by FMC and a change in payment behavior at Vamed in Q1. So Q2 was a very strong cash flow quarter, getting us EUR 610 million with a margin of 11%. 
The top of the slide, Kabi. The EUR 173 million cash flow, a margin of almost 14%, which is below that. The EUR 128 million from HELIOS are almost 10% in Q2. So in both cases, margin was above the respective LTM average group CapEx of 5.5%. 
Now back to the bottom of the slide. That is very much in line with my expectations and I'm therefore more than pleased with the LTM free cash flow margin of 4.6%. 
Let's conclude with an update on our guidance on Slides 12 and 13 for the business segments on 12 first. So as discussed for Kabi's organic sales growth, we saw first effects from underlying pickup and easing comps. So we are confident with our guidance range of 4% to 6% and pretty much the same is true for Kabi's margin with 16.7% in the first half. We're still in the lower part of our guidance range. However, as discussed when going through the individual regions, we have good reason to believe in a pickup in the second half. 
At the same time, I want to slightly caution you reaching the, say, the upper 1/3 of our guidance range. That will require a reversal of the current easing trend for drug shortages.
Now for HELIOS. Organic growth in the first half was within our guidance range. And as discussed, we already saw patient growth picking up and I'm optimistic that this will stabilize at the Q2 level -- around the Q2 level for the second half. 
Now we had promised a reliable guidance for the Rhön portfolio for today. And with a -- and given that we now have clarity on Wiesbaden, we are also comfortable providing that. So we are expecting around EUR 1.8 billion as a sales contribution from the Rhön hospitals for 2014 and that number already incorporates the intra-year first-time consolidation effects. And your run rate is higher than expected currently at approximately EUR 1.9 billion. 
For EBIT of the Rhön portfolio, given the so far successful integration and also the performance in the second quarter, we believe that for HELIOS as a whole, we should be able to accomplish EUR 540 million to EUR 560 million in this year. 
Now if you bear in mind what the original guidance for the old HELIOS portfolio has been, namely, EUR 390 million to EUR 410  million, that implies a margin of -- an EBIT of approximately EUR 150 million for the Rhön portfolio, given the EUR 40 million that we've turned in, in 2 [ph], notwithstanding the first-time consolidation of Wiesbaden from Q3, that should be achievable. 
For Vamed, notwithstanding the negative growth for the first half year, given the full order book and given the typical volatility that we do see in the project business, after a thorough review, we feel comfortable confirming our growth guidance for both the top line and for EBIT. The backlog gives us substantial comfort here. 
So finally, for the group. After constant currency top line growth of 13% in Q2, very well in line with our expectations and with the added revenue contribution from FMC's acquisitions, we do feel comfortable taking our top line guidance from 12% to 15% to now 14% to 16%. 
As explained when going through these individual businesses, be it Kabi, be it HELIOS, be it Vamed, we do expect a pickup of growth in the second half, again, with a higher contribution from acquisitions. 
Also I did talk about a good reason to believe in growing EBIT in the second half and given everything that I said also about interest and tax, we do feel very comfortable with our guidance for earnings after-tax growth for the full year. Also here, I would expect at least a slight mitigation of the so far negative foreign exchange effect in the full year. 
That concludes my comments. 
Maybe as a last and final housekeeping item, you may have seen that we want to implement our stock split by tomorrow. The new share will be trading -- is expected to trade as for Monday. This may be a good time to adjust your target prices. Just kidding.
Mark and I will now be happy to take your questions. Thank you very much.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: I have 3 this morning or this afternoon. Maybe we take can them in kind. The first question I was -- or sort of comment that I was hoping you could clarify is the volume growth at HELIOS because looking at your organic growth rate today, it did actually decelerate from 4% to 3%, Q1 to Q2. So was there a mix that you're seeing in terms of volume  improved but mix worsened? Or what else might we be missing about the improvement in HELIOS?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Veronika, this is Mark and I expected that question and it bears some explanation. I think the good news is that the admissions are -- because the admissions are the source of everything, but especially at the beginning of the year, like the first half, there's always a price/mix component and that comes with how the price increases are implemented across the states in Germany. Because in addition to the DRG inflator that you're all familiar with, what basically happens state-by-state is that the hospital budget, when it comes to allowed case numbers and pricing and case mix index are being negotiated. And then when you reach agreement, all of this gets up applied retroactively to the beginning of that year. Now between the years, sometimes we conclude more of those negotiations in Q1, sometimes more them in Q2, but most of that happens in the first half. And so what happened in the first quarter of this year is that we had a whole lot of those agreements already put in place, and hence, we saw basically a price/mix impact of plus 4%, which bailed us out from that negative patient admissions growth. And in the second quarter, there were fewer left to be done and so were the price mix component of plus 2% and that squares that with what you saw. And that is even though we had stronger patient admissions, the organic sales growth came in a bit lower in Q1 compared -- in Q2 compared to Q1.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: That's great. And Mark, any views on what we might see for DRG inflator heading into 2015? Anything you'd like to -- any speculation or your views that you can share with us on that?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Pretty hard to speculate on it. I think we'll all know by October. I think everything points to pretty positive developments and -- but again, I'm sure until we put out to the public in October is pretty hard to know. But it is noteworthy that a lot of the public and not-for-profit hospitals, even with very generous reimbursement increases we've been seeing last year and this year, were struggling. And so there's a lot of pressure here to be somewhat generous for 2015 as well.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Understood. My second question is Rhön-Klinikum. And Stephan, if I remember correctly, you said that we might start seeing some of the savings from the restructuring as early as fourth quarter. Given the timing delays, should we be changing our assumptions about that?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Veronika, you may refer to some earlier statements. As of today, I was pointing to margin increases already right from the beginning. And therefore, we are seeing the first synergy, cost synergy, benefits already. What Mark, in his prepared comments, was referring to was that we would like to confirm the absolute run rate of EUR 85 million. However, that given the delay in getting the transaction closed, the time when you would reach that run rate, in all likelihood, is also going to be slightly delayed.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Understood. And my last  question is just on the Kabi margin development in Europe. And Stephan,  I know that you used have lowered our expectations for that at Q1 and it seems like Q2, as you pointed out, improved a bit. But I'm just wondering, if you look at that European business, how is was tracking versus your expectations from the beginning of the year? And if it is below or ahead, what has been the key moving parts that has moved your expectations throughout the year for the European Kabi business?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Frankly, Veronika, it was pretty much in line with our revised expectations from Q1. No big secret that we had hoped for a somewhat earlier stabilization of the HES business and that did not materialize or it only materialized now in Q2. That is what we were also hoping for when we were commenting on Q1. And therefore, Q2 is pretty much in line with the expectations from, say, 3 months ago.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: I had 3 question, as well. The first one, a nice general one. Just on the network agreement that you signed with Rhön and [indiscernible] potentially  some other facilities in Germany, I'm just wondering if you can give a bit more color on when you might expect that start having a benefit on the business and how that is likely to manifest itself through the revenue line, through the cost, or perhaps through a mix of both. Just some more clarity about the potential magnitude and the timing of that agreement would be very helpful. Secondly, just a question for Stephan, I guess. Just to clarify what you said in FX. Are you expecting FX to be mitigated in the second half in the sense it just goes away as being a headwind? Or are you expecting to swing around to being a modest tailwind and therefore offset some of the pressure you felt in the first half? And then the third question, which potentially a bit more it involved. If I go back to the days when the news about that Kabi Grand Island  warning letter broke, the general sort of sense seemed to be that launch schedules for 2014 wouldn't really be impacted, but perhaps then, by 2015, things might be a bit more problematic. I don't know whether you actually publicly espoused that deal, that's just the one we will jump to. But what potentially might have changed between the initial receipt of this warning letter now that makes the launch schedule for 2014 less problematic as regards to this warning letter? What have you done to potentially mitigate the impact there?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content:
Yes, thanks, Tom. I'll be happy to address your first and your third question and then Stephan will take the second one. On the network agreement, again we hope to get going with the full network, so that's including Rhön and Asklepios in the fourth quarter. That doesn't mean you'll see any meaningful impact yet and I would expect that this is something that's going to grow fairly modestly over the first several quarters and maybe start to show a somewhat more meaningful impact from 2016 or so. Remember, when we introduced the deal to you, the Rhön deal and the whole network deal, we told you that, near term, our focus was on getting the cost synergies and that any strategy piece really would require few years to exactly show any effect. As and when you see the effect, I think it will happen through the revenue line because this should steer more additional cases in our direction. And so I would treat it as, for now, as the reinsurance policy on the 3% to 5% organic sales growth target, but not really build it in with any additional upside in your model. And then we'll keep updating you once we started the rollout and how it's going and what to expect down the road. On Grand Island, yes, there was a good observation, good catch and there's a very simple answer to it. And that is, of course, this -- what we said at that time refers to the launch schedules that exist at the time. And as a matter of precaution, of course, anything at the time that we could shift elsewhere, we have. I mean, that was basically standard operating procedure since we didn't know how long exactly it would take to resolve all matters with FDA. And hence, we feel good about the statement now regarding 2015, but it does square and is consistent with what we said at the time that the launch schedules have existed at the end of 2011 or early 2012 we're actually more geared towards Grand Island. Keep in mind, the average approval time for a generic in the U.S. right now is at about 27 to 30 months. And so for anything that was far along, there was no point in changing the destination of
 where we manufacture the product. But for anything that was still at early, it did make sense to make those shifts.

sections.speaker: Thomas Jones
sections.content: Great. And just for clarity, where have they been shifted to? I mean, you've just recently sold one manufacturing facility. You've comfortably got adequate capacity to make this and/or the other products or potentially might have you have to rein back on production of some perhaps less-profitable product to replace them with the more profitable, newer products?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Well, remember, the other key U.S. location is Melrose Park near Chicago, which has a stellar FDA record and so that's our other cornerstone when it comes to the U.S. manufacturing strategy. The one that we sold, that announcement that came through a few weeks ago, there was always a nonstrategic plan that gave us some additional validation capacity, but it was not something that was meant to be built up as a major hub like a Grand Island or Melrose Park.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Tom, really, your foreign exchange question, as you know, in that respect, dollar-euro is the elephant in the room. And I have a  euro-dollar chart just in front of me and what it shows is that after a peak as far as dollar weakness is concerned, just at the end of Q2 last year at around $1.28. The dollar was gradually trending up to reach about $1.38 at the year end. And the current level of approximately $1.34, that was reached at about mid-third quarter. And therefore, if we take the current spot as the best proxy for the future development this year, we should actually have some foreign exchange tailwind for the second half of Q3 and for Q4. That is what I was referring to and therefore I would expect that on this extreme currency headwind in Q2, we should see a mitigation going forward.

sections.speaker: Alexander Kleban
sections.content: Maybe just 2 for me. One, can you give your perspective on the Hikma-Bedford Labs deal in terms of just U.S. injectables? Were you involved in terms of bidding and ultimately why you were not part of that deal? And then second, you gave the run rate for HES. Just wondering if you could give us some sense of gross margin and geographic breakdown on that as well.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes, thanks. Asking for your understanding that I wouldn't want to comment in detail on the transaction involving 2 competitors. We felt very good about our posture in the U.S. so I don't think that this transaction will have any impact on us directly. Regarding HES, we didn't provide specific gross margin numbers other than to say that it is a fairly profitable product for us, and I think you guessed it already, given the HES sales reduction that occurred from last year to this year and then some of the corresponding margin reduction that you saw, particularly for Europe, which is a strong HES market. So it's quite a profitable product, but we've not publicly disclosed any gross margin numbers.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: Actually, a related question. Firstly, can you give us a view on -- or an update, if you are able to, on your interest or not in Danone's nutrition business and your stance on that? And secondly, on nutrition, you have recently launched a nutrition product into the U.S. market. Can you give us an update on how you're doing with those launches? You previously guided on some sales synergies of nutrition in the U.S. Can you update us on how you're doing there, please?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes, no further comment on Danone nutrition situation. In fact, I simply don't know what exactly is going on there. Regarding the U.S. nutrition market, we received some first initial approvals, but not the key one yet that we're waiting for and that's our three-chamber bag and I think that's the one that we believe is a real door opener for the U.S. market. So rather than counting the revenue target from this first approval that we received earlier, let's wait for the end of this year, early next year when we hope to get the three-chamber bag approval and we'll then launch that, which, I think, will have some more meaningful impact. So the old estimate, that EUR 50 million to EUR 70 million additional revenue, is once we really have the necessary approvals, I think that still stands. But as we explained in earlier calls, getting that approved has been quite a bit delayed. Some of this was intentional because we focused our regulatory groups on other opportunities, including Propofol and other imported products into the U.S. but some of it was also simply more back and forth with FDA than we expected at the beginning.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: Mark, and in terms of the three-chamber bag, I mean, do you think you now have visibility that you -- you can get approval at latest by first quarter next year, to your point about issues you had?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes, I do think that 2015 is a good estimate. And then from that point, when we have the approval, I think the -- within basically 1 to 2 years, ramping to that EUR 50 million to EUR 70 million still is a meaningful estimate.

sections.speaker: Edward Ridley-Day
sections.content: Very good. Just a quick follow-up on the -- your nutrition business generally then. Do you maybe -- do you feel confident in the way you're positioned in nutrition? Or are there actually some areas where you would actually be interested in strengthening?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: I feel very confident in the sense that we are the only company that has leading market positions in enteral and enteral -- sorry, enteral and parenteral. And given that you would always want to leave the choice with the doctor and that any particular patient at any point in time might actually migrate back and forth between these 2. I think to offer the full portfolio is important, just like with FMC offering hemodialysis and peritoneal hemodialysis.  So having strong positions in both and not just traded product from someone else, I think, is important. Obviously, lot of our nutrition business is centered on Europe and then I think we rolled it out early into Asia Pacific and into Latin America. I think North America is still a bit of a new territory for us. In North America, when you look at enteral and parenteral, I think parenteral works quite closely to how it works in Europe. Enteral is more of a retail market and therefore a little bit further away from us and so that would not be our first priority.

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: I have a couple of questions to the group guidance. You've increased the guidance for the HELIOS business and how does it translate to the group profit would be my first question. And on the HELIOS business as well, since you have a better handle on the acquisition, could you give us an idea what the case mix changes with the new addition to your portfolio, the improvement potential and the margin expansion opportunities from the acquisition, just purely on the case mix. And on Kabi, could you -- you talked a lot about the China price cuts, U.S. product shortages. Can you give us some more idea or more color on the magnitude of the China price cuts and also the implied organic growth rate that you expect in the second half where exactly it would come from?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Martin, at the face of it, we were increasing the HELIOS guidance. But obviously, when we provided guidance at the outset of the year, what was built into the group net income guidance was an internal assumption as to the profitability of the Rhön portfolio, yes? And we were saying that we only feel comfortable to share that assumption with  you once we knew when -- which major hospital would really be consolidated by us. That's why we were saying full reliable guidance for the enlarged HELIOS only as part of our Q2 communication, i.e., today. And therefore, as I explained in my prepared comments, what it implies is an approximately EUR 150 million EBIT contribution from the newly acquired Rhön hospitals. I made comments about the corresponding interest charge. And as you know, we are exempt in German hospitals from trade tax, and therefore, HELIOS is operating with a tax rate somewhere between 18% and 19%. Does that answer your question?

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: No, I assumed obviously that you would have baked that into the guidance already, but I was wondering how much it changed from your original assumptions and if you can give us some color there.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Martin, the -- as we said -- as Mark said, the hospital portfolio is operating very much in line with our expectations and we have found good areas to substantiate our original assumption that we had. Remind me of your second question, please?

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: I asked about the case mix index change since you have a better handle of all these hospitals, how it changed from old HELIOS to the integrated group now? What's the improvement on the case mix index that you see or the diversity? And what's the margin -- how does that translate into margin expansion or contraction? Just purely on the case mix, not on all the other levers that you have obviously in integration opportunities, just on the case mix itself as it stands.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: To the best of my knowledge, no material difference, which means neither the risk of all the billing, no substantial upside potential. This is very much in line with -- or has been very much in line with what we would do as well, and therefore, this is fully baked into our medium-term assumptions.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Martin, this Mark. Let me just build on that. First of all, pulling back what Stephan said, keep in mind, in the past, we never really guided by the case mix index. And the assumption was always that if you have a more attractive one, you probably also face higher costs and more medical complexity to deal with this because the more complex your procedures, the more backup you really need. And so that's why with the EBIT targets for our hospitals, we never differentiated by the CMI.

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: Yes, but the effect is that they are all different. And the heart [indiscernible] have significantly higher EBITDA than the general hospitals. So it must be the effect of the case mix.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: That's what I was saying. It has a significant higher value there. But then, of course, you also -- when you look at that facility, it's  more complex facility, where absolute top-notch, state-of-the-art heart medicine is being practiced. That cost structure looks quite different from the average 200- or 300-bed low-cost hospitals somewhere else. So that's what I'm saying, I guess, when it comes to guiding expectations on EBIT margins, that's not really relevant to us. That center has to fulfill the same EBIT margin targets as anyone else.

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: And on the Kabi business, I was wondering whether you can quantify the impact of the China price cuts since you mentioned it's off in the last calls and the organic growth with expectations that are implied in the second half? Where exactly did it come from? How much does it come from cross-selling? How much from your product launches? So a little bit of color there would be appreciated.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Yes, I think the price cuts that went into effect last February, we basically at the time, talked about exactly less than 10% average impact on our China Kabi portfolio. So if you want absolute numbers, we applied that to revenues between EUR 400 million and EUR 500 million to get a sense of what the impact was, and that's a onetime step-down that basically occurred as of February 2013. There have been no subsequent cuts and we have no information about more cuts looming. And so once the year-over-year negative quarterly effect was washed out with Q1 this year, what you saw now is organic growth perking back up. And so we expect high single digit or something in the vicinity of that for the remainder of this year.

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: Okay, that's very helpful. And the very last question is relating to FMC, just maybe I will ask here. If you look at the cash conversion rates, and I appreciate there may have been a one-off in the last quarter, but there is a real trend of cash conversion rate coming down, if you look at this on a 12 months rolling basis comparing to adjusted EBIT that the company reports. You had it at 60% almost in Q4, now it's at 40% if you do it on a 12 months rolling basis. So what's going on there with this trend? And maybe could elaborate a little bit on this, whether we should expect this to continue in the -- for the remainder of the year

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Martin, I'd love to help, but there's a reason we have these calls back-to-back and so I think that's best addressed to the CFO, Mike Brosnan in the FMC call.

sections.speaker: Ian Douglas-Pennant
sections.content: Just a quick one on Kabi margins. I'm sure everyone's very happy with the North America margin, especially. But I mean can you just explain to us a little bit more about how you are managing to maintain that margin at such a high level even as Propofol declined? I mean, is it all just a kind of a large number of different injectable generics, specialty kind of stories? Or is there anything else you can point to there? And on the European margin, again very happy to hear that you expect margins to pick up on the second half of this year. But maybe you could talk a little bit on kind of how you're doing that because I assume that the margin -- the depressed margins at the moment, or at least, in part due to HES, which I don't think will recover. So are you changing anything there? And then just kind of second more general question. I mean, very  impressive track record on increasing net profit every quarter, but however many -- it was 46 quarters that I think you said. Does that affect your decision-making everyday? Or do you just literally look at the end of the quarter and are happy to see that it happened again?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Ian, as far as Kabi's margins in North America are concerned, I was saying that the mid-30s, given the dilution effect of Fenwal's medical devices business, translate into a margin in the high 30s for our IV drug business. And there, frankly, we have been tracking for quite a while. Actually, at the time when we were acquiring the APP business, it was lower. When the drug shortage situation was even more tense than it is now, we were also well tracking above 40%. This is quite a vast and diversified portfolio of individual drugs. At some point, we were saying that we have approximately, what was it, 5 or 6 drugs that were exceeding a revenue of EUR 50 million annualized. And then the remainder is really a broad portfolio of individual drugs. And given the overall characteristics of that business, the margin level has traditionally been there. And also going forward, even if the drug shortages receded completely, I would expect a normalized margin level in the mid-30s for the IV drug part of the business.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: On the net income, since I mentioned that in my part of the presentation, let me just say, believe it or not, I didn't pay attention until someone this winter told me, congratulations, 40 consecutive quarters. And so given that this is a fairly unsettled time in a time when even a whole lot of companies with established track records are disappointing, I felt it's worth pointing out the enormous stability in our business model. But it certainly does not affect our day-to-day decision making. It's simply something that I started to pay attention to after it was pointed out to me. And I think the group of companies that have -- who have delivered that for more than 40 times in a row is pretty limited.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Notwithstanding what Mark said and it's fully backing that up, we're all pretty much into earnings growth day-to-day.

sections.speaker: Ian Douglas-Pennant
sections.content: Great. And I'm sorry, on the European Kabi margin indiscernible]

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Look, that is very much operating leverage coming from top line growth and given the assumption that the HELIOS business is going to stabilize in the second half, coupled with a few IV drug launches also in the second half.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content: Okay. And with that, thanks for joining the call. I appreciate your time and your questions, and goodbye.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Thank you.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Thank you.

sections.speaker: Birgit Grund
sections.content:
Good afternoon in Europe, good morning in the U.S. Welcome to our third quarter earnings conference call and webcast. Thank you very much to everyone joining the call. I'm very pleased to introduce to you Mark Schneider, CEO of Fresenius; and Stephan Sturm, CFO.
Before we get started, I'd like to say that today's call is being recorded, and I'd also like to draw your attention to the forward-looking statement and disclaimer wording on Page 2 of the presentation. This cautionary language also applies to the presentation and comments to be made today.
Mark Schneider will now start with a general overview, followed by Stephan Sturm, he will take you through the financials. And afterwards, management is prepared to answer your questions.
And with that, let me hand it over to Mark. Mark, please?

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content:
Thank you, Birgit, and a warm welcome to our conference call participants today. As always, we appreciate your interest in our company. We are pleased to report a strong quarter in all our business segments. It is particularly reassuring to see that we are gathering momentum across the board, in line with our predictions earlier this year.
Birgit has pointed out the safe harbor language to you, so let's move right to Page 3, which summarizes our financial results for the quarter and 9-month period in 2014. Please note that on this page and throughout the presentation and our investor news, we have added single quarter data for improved convenience and clarity.
In the third quarter we achieved sales of EUR 5,978,000,000, EBIT of EUR 820 million and net income of EUR 281 million. You can clearly see that all growth rates for the individual quarter are above the growth rates for the respective 9-month period.
Page 4 continues on that theme, with every single growth rate for Q3 above their respective 9-month period. I would like to point your attention to Fresenius Medical Care's improved EBIT growth and the improving sales growth for Fresenius Kabi. Clearly, it has been a successful and well-balanced quarter, which gives us confidence with regards to our full year group expectations.
Before turning to Fresenius Kabi, HELIOS and Vamed in more detail, a brief word on Fresenius Medical Care. Q3 confirmed the trend we have started to see in the second quarter, and that is solid operational improvements, coupled with meaningful progress on the company's strategic objectives that were outlined in this year's Capital Market Day. After a difficult year in 2013, it is pleasing to see that the company is making good on its growth and efficiency objectives.
Turning to Page 5 in Fresenius Kabi. We're now able to give you a more specific update regarding our Grand Island facility in Upstate New York. I know that this is an area of concern for some of you, and I am pleased to report major progress here. Our Grand Island facility was inspected by 3 FDA investigators the week beginning October 20. We received a 2-page notice of observations, also known as Form 483, consisting of 3 observations, each with 2 or 3 examples. We intend to respond to FDA in a timely way and to take corrective and preventive actions to fully address FDA's concerns.
Since our last inspection at this facility in January 2013, we have made substantial efforts to improve our quality system so that it meets FDA's expectations as well as our own. We believe management at the facility has brought renewed energy and focus to our efforts. The results of the last inspection reflect our progress. Based on the observations that were received and comments made during the inspection by the investigators, we're optimistic that FDA will note the improvement in our quality systems and compliance, and classify the compliance status as VAI, or voluntary action indicated. This classification would permit new products manufactured at the facility to be approved.
Until FDA has a chance to review our responses to the observations, however, we cannot be certain that FDA will agree that sufficient progress has been made. What I can assure you is that we will continue to review and improve our quality systems and facilities and that this is a top priority.
The drug shortage situation in the U.S. market is developing in line with our expectations. We're seeing a gradual rebalancing of supply and demand. As of the end of October, there were 67 products on the FDA drug shortage list, with 21 of them marketed by Fresenius Kabi. This is essentially unchanged from the situation at the end of July. We continue to import saline solution into the U.S. market under FDA regulatory discretion, and have recorded sales of about EUR 5 million year-to-date.
The other good news is that our Kabi emerging markets have seen a very strong third quarter, confirming that Kabi's emerging market growth story is fully intact. Since most emerging market countries are pursuing mid- to long-term plans to improve health care coverage to their populations, any short-term weakness in these markets when it comes to industrial or consumer products does not affect our business.
Finally, I would like to confirm that sales for our blood volume expander HES has stabilized at the Q2 level, in line with our expectations mentioned earlier this year.
Let's move to HELIOS on Page 6. One area of concern from earlier this year was our admissions development. I believe the 2% admissions growth this quarter should put these concerns to rest. It also gives us lots of confidence regarding our full year 3% to 5% organic revenue growth guidance.
Integration of the acquired Rhön hospitals is a key focus area this year. We're now in a position to reduce our integration cost estimate to a range of EUR 60 million to EUR 80 million in total, split roughly 50-50 between 2014 and 2015.
Even though the integration cost estimate is reduced, we're in a position to fully confirm our annual synergy guidance of EUR 85 million starting from 2015. So while the integration of more than 40 facilities is a mammoth project and takes significant work, it is pleasing to see that until now, it has gone extremely well. 
At this time of the year, there's a lot of focus on 2015 hospital reimbursement. I'm pleased to report that a 2.53% reimbursement increase has been established for that year. In addition, as part of the new legislation, which is still subject to confirmation by both chambers of German parliament, the 0.8% surcharge that applies to German acute care hospitals will stay in place in 2015. Net of some new adjustments for case volume above budget, we expect a positive annual effect of about EUR 10 million for HELIOS' sales and EBIT. The reimbursement increase and the new legislation are fully confirming the strong reimbursement trends we have witnessed for the past 2 years.
Vamed has won a major project and service contract for a German university hospital this quarter, in conjunction with a consortium partner. The initial hospital modernization project of more than EUR 500 million is followed by a 30-year technical management services contract, bringing the total value to about EUR 1.7 billion. Even adjusting for the consortium partner share of 50%, this is one of the largest projects Vamed has ever won.
On the minus side, I would like to point you to project delays that Vamed is facing this year in Russia and the Ukraine. As a result, organic sales growth for 2014 is likely to fall below the 5% to 10% range we guided you to earlier. However, we're in a position to fully confirm our 5% to 10% EBIT growth expectation.
So in summary, this was a strong quarter with growing momentum and building on the solid improvements we have started to see in the second quarter this year. As you know, financial market credibility is very important to us. We are particularly pleased that the so-called trust us items we had discussed earlier this year, such as growth being back loaded, the development of hospital admissions, returning emerging market growth and the stabilization of HES sales, are really happening in line with our forecasts.
So on that positive note, let me hand it over to Stephan for a more detailed discussion of our financials.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content:
Thank you, Mark. Good afternoon, good morning. You just heard it. We saw the expected further acceleration of growth in Q3, plus an outstanding cash flow development. And so I'm very happy with the quarter overall.
Let's go straight to the group P&L on Slide 8, where sales came in just below EUR 6 billion in Q3 to reach EUR 16.7 billion year-to-date. The 18% growth in actual currency is up a mighty 10 points from the second quarter for the 3 reasons I have mentioned in our Q2 call: one, the expected pickup of organic growth; two, the growing contribution from acquisitions; and three, the still negative but now mitigated currency effect. A few details. One, organic growth. 6% in Q3, up from just above 3% in the prior quarter, driven by all business segments. FMC up 2 points to almost 7%; Kabi up 1.25%; HELIOS up even 3 points to 6%; and Vamed with a massive 19 point swing to plus 7%. And this is more than just the effect of easing comps. If we strip out those areas where we had an extraordinary impact last year, we're also showing very decent organic growth in the mid-single digits.
On growth from acquisitions, secondly, that was 14% in Q3 versus the 10% in the prior quarter. That sequential pickup is driven by the first-time consolidation of the former Rhön hospitals HSK Wiesbaden and Cuxhaven. And over above those HELIOS effect, it was FMC, now consolidating most of its recently announced acquisitions.
And finally, the third driver, currency impact, still at a negative minus 2 points in Q3. But that's a meaningful improvement from the minus 5% in the second quarter. In our earnings call, end of July, I was only referring to the known dollar weakness in the second half of last year. I had no visibility of the dollar strength that kicked in about 3 months ago. But combined, that led to an even stronger mitigation than expected. So for Q3 year-on-year, average euro-dollar rate was virtually identical. And it was primarily the Latin American currencies behind the remaining negative currency effect.
So for the fourth quarter, given ongoing dollar strength, the currency effect should turn positive; group organic growth should be broadly in line with Q3, again, driven by both an underlying improvement as well as by moderated comps; and the contribution from acquisitions, that should also remain largely unchanged. So constant currency growth, expect that in the Q3 ballpark. But reported growth should increase from the 11% we're showing year-to-date.
On to EBIT, and that reached EUR 820 million in the third quarter, up 10% at constant currency. A meaningful improvement from the minus 6% in Q1 and the plus 5% in Q2. Ex FMC, EBIT growth was even higher, 14% in Q3 at constant currency or a year-to-date growth of 8%.
Sequential progress pretty much across all business segments. Kabi posting positive EBIT growth after 2 quarters of decline, given the tough comps they were up against. Also Vamed, strongly reversing the Q2 decrease; and HELIOS with yet another quarter of more than 40% EBIT growth. For Q4, we certainly plan to increase group EBIT further. And when we're at plus 3% constant currency growth year-to-date, that rate should move up for the full year.
Net interest was EUR 148 million in Q3, taking us to EUR 431 million year-to-date. So no major change from Q2, which is quite an achievement, given FMC's incremental acquisition debt and the U.S. dollar strength. But that was largely offset by further reduced interest rates. Now for the fourth quarter, I expect a pickup of interest expenses mainly due to FMC's acquisitions and the refinancing of some of that debt. But when last time, I was pointing you to the bottom of the EUR 610 million to EUR 630 million range for the full year, by now I'd say we're unlikely to be above EUR 600 million.
The tax rate was back to normal at 29.3% in Q3, taking us to 29.5% year-to-date. And so for the full year, I'd like to confirm our guidance of a tax rate just above 29%.
Net income, before integration costs and disposal gains, was EUR 281 million in Q3, up 5% at constant currency. So also for the bottom line, we were able to accelerate growth. So we gained 1 point from the half-year, and are now at 4% growth year-to-date, well within our guidance range.
For reported growth, we also saw a negative foreign exchange effect run out of steam, down to 1 point. And that is where I expect it to stay also for the full year.
With that, let's move over to Kabi on Slide 9. Total EBIT at the bottom of the page came in at EUR 223 million in the third quarter at constant currency. That's an increase of 1% year-over-year, a major improvement versus the minus 13% in Q1 and the minus 5% in Q2. And again, this is both an underlying improvement and a moderation of the year-over-year comps. China price cuts past the anniversary already, but given that HES only stabilized earlier this year, we’re still up against tough comps. And also the gradual normalization of drug shortages in North America is weighing on Kabi's EBIT growth.
But if we strip out those areas, Kabi's EBIT growth is actually very decent, and that bodes well for growth rates once we're truly comparing apples with apples again.
On the margin. With 17.2% in Q3, we're now at 16.9% year-to-date. So a small progress from the half year. I had hinted to that during our Q2 call, and I had pointed out that anything north of 17.5% will require a reversal of the normalization trend for drug shortages that hasn't materialized and doesn't seem to be in the cards for now. So we're basically confirming our earlier statement by specifying our guidance to approximately 17%.
Onto the regions, where in Europe, top of the slide, the 15% margin in Q3 was slightly down sequentially, primarily driven by Russia. But it helped take the year-to-date margin to 14.8%. And for the fourth quarter, I remain optimistic about a meaningful margin increase, and that should take the full year margin well above 15%.
In North America, I had projected margin erosion to the mid-30s for the second half, driven by further easing of drug shortages. That actually materialized. And so the 35.8% margin in Q3 is in line with our expectations.
In the emerging markets, we saw the projected sequential pickup. Margin plus 140 basis points quarter-on-quarter to 17.6%, absolute EBIT plus 23% quarter-on-quarter and 20% year-over-year. For the full year, I don't expect the margin to be materially different from where we are year-to-date.
Corporate and R&D expense was EUR 59 million in Q3, so slightly below my guidance of a run rate in the low- to mid-60s. For Q4, my best guess right now is a repeat of the Q3 level.
Now over to HELIOS on Slide 10, where sales reached EUR 1.36 billion in Q3, very healthy organic growth in combination with the first-time consolidations I mentioned earlier.
Organic patient growth is truly back on track, and even slightly accelerated to 2%, following the turnaround in Q2, a major contribution from price mix this past quarter, taking organic sales growth to 6% in Q3 and up 1 point to 4% year-to-date. So we're at the midpoint of our guidance range now. And whilst I'm very happy with that 6% quarter, I don't expect a repeat in Q4, neither should you. There was a bit of a base effect from a softish Q3 last year. And as Mark explained, as part of our Q2 call, mild quarterly fluctuations are nothing unusual for HELIOS. So my best guess is that we'll maintain that 4% year-to-date level also for the full year.
Total EBIT at the bottom of the page came in at EUR 147 million, nice progress from the EUR 136 million in Q2. And also margin wise, we saw a further pickup, 30 basis points sequentially to reach 10.8% in Q3. And whilst HSK Wiesbaden was not materially negative, it was clearly margin dilutive.
So looking at the established portfolio, I don't think you should read anything into the slight year-on-year margin erosion. Sequentially, the margin is up by 60 basis points. And we also saw a sequential increase of the absolute EBIT contribution from the acquired Rhön hospitals, up 10% from EUR 40 million to EUR 44 million. I think that's quite an achievement, given the consolidation of HSK in Wiesbaden. So you heard it from Mark, all in all, we're very pleased with the Rhön integration process.
Move on to cash flow on Slide 11, and starting with the group at the bottom of the slide. I want to confirm, from my perspective, it was an outstanding quarter. Operating cash flow of EUR 945 million represents a stellar margin of 15.8%, substantially ahead of the LTM average of 11.1%. I believe that's quite an achievement, given the onetime burdens at FMC and Vamed in the first quarter.
This quarter, strong contributions pretty much across-the-board. Kabi's EUR 217 million in Q3 represents a margin of 16.8%. And HELIOS also delivered just below EUR 200 million at a margin of 14.6%, driven into area [ph] by then reducing the DSOs back down to 40 days.
CapEx of 5.6% year-to-date is well in line with our expectations. And therefore, the LTM free cash flow margin of 5.5% is more towards the upper end of our historic range.
Let's conclude with an update on our guidance on Slides 12 and 13. For Kabi, on 12. The organic sales growth, after an overall solid Q3, I'd expect a pickup in Europe, no major changes in North America and the emerging markets. And in combination, that will take us into our guidance range of 4% to 6%. And whilst the upper end appears a bit of a stretch right now, I firmly believe that the bottom end is safe.
For Kabi's margin, a not dissimilar picture, expect a pickup in Europe, limited visibility in North America. I don't see much of an effect from the emerging markets, and a bit of margin support from stable corporate expenses relative to a growing top line. And that should give us a margin of approximately 17% for the full year.
Now for HELIOS, I was already making a statement about the 6% and then not being a proxy. And that I firmly believe in us making it to the middle, at least, of that 3% to 5% organic growth rate guidance. Likewise, I'd like to confirm our EBIT range for the new HELIOS portfolio, EUR 540 million to EUR 560 million. Given that we're just shy of EUR 400 million year-to-date and recorded just below EUR 150 million in the third quarter, it basically takes another Q3 to take us into that guidance range, so I'm not worried about that.
You heard Mark make the comments about Vamed's slightly reduced growth expectations for this year. We’re talking about project delays, no project cancellations. And again, we're very bullish on the 5% to 10% EBIT growth for Vamed this year.
For the group, on Slide 13. You heard me talk about sales growth, and I would expect a 20% plus reported sales growth in the fourth quarter. Constant currency, I'm not worried about the bottom end of that 14% to 16% range. Again, that is going to be -- Q4 is going to be in the ballpark of Q3.
And for net income, given where we are right now, I also feel very confident about that 2% to 5% net income growth. And you heard me with the expectation, that we're going to see a somewhat reduced currency -- negative currency effect for the full year.
All in all, a very pleasing quarter, both P&L and cash flow wise. Thanks for your attention so far. Mark and I will now be happy to take your questions. Thank you.

sections.speaker: Ian Douglas-Pennant
sections.content: Just a quick one. On the North American margin for Kabi, slightly weaker than maybe I have expected, although I appreciate it's within your guidance range. Still, I wondered whether you could make a comment on price inflation for your IV portfolio there. And whether that's having an impact on your margins? And then secondly, on your clinical nutrition business. Good acceleration in that business in the last 2 quarters. I wonder whether you could talk about what's driving that. And whether it might be the new products that you've spoken about before?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: It's Stephan. For the North American margin, as you just said, it's within our guidance range. It is a very differentiated picture across the various drugs that we're seeing in that market. And I cannot make a general statement across the entire portfolio. When others are talking about price increases, again, we don't see that across the board, and it may be individual categories that drive that. Again, for the fourth quarter, I would not expect a meaningful change to that margin picture. For clinical nutrition, I would -- it is not the new product in North America. That was only just about launched, and it is way too early to make any comments about that. It is much rather the pickup in China that has been driving this product category for Kabi.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: I have 3, if I may, please. The first one is on the Kabi revenue guidance that you've issued. And Stephan, I appreciate that you're guiding us towards the lower end. But looking at where you are year-to-date, which is at 3%, it's going to be quite a stretch to get to 4% for the full year. So if you can share with us, which specific categories you think might drive the acceleration, that would be quite helpful. The second question I have is just, given the downgrade that you have, or -- I don't want to call it downgrade because it's not, it's within your range. But given that you're now expecting 17% guide -- margin for Kabi, does that alter how we should be thinking about it medium term? And my last question is on Grand Island, and how much confidence and visibility you have into being able to get this VAI status, given that you've been going back and forth with the FDA on this issue for a number of years now.

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Veronica, I don't think there is a lot to add given the granularity that I've already provided for the revenue guidance. I do believe that the major growth driver in this fourth quarter at Kabi is going to be Europe. And don't forget, over and above the underlying development, that we are -- continue to see weakening comps from the last -- from last year. So I am -- as I said, 6% is a stretch, 4%, I feel very comfortable with. Margin wise, look, we have seen a few onetime effects over the course of this year. And we were referring to the price cuts in China. And obviously, the closer you are to the actual point of implementation, the more difficult it is to offset the effect with volume growth. We had an impact from Russia. Your guess is as good as mine as to how that is going to play out. As far as the drug shortage effect receding, yes, we have seen that. But over time, and I guess, Mark is going to take up your question on Grand Island. Over time, we would expect new drug launches to be a more important driver than drug shortages. And we remain optimistic about the launches in 2015 [ph] through to 2007 (sic) [2017]. I remember you were asking me the question in February. And our view here remains unchanged. As far as the long-term perspectives of our Kabi business are concerned, we remain optimistic. For 2015, you know our policy. It's too early to give you specific guidance. We will update that. In all likelihood, we will also provide you with a new medium-term guidance reflecting the new realities in terms of drug shortage and product mix at the occasion of the full year results in February. But do I see a step down in margin from where we are right now? No, I can't see that.

sections.speaker: Ulf Schneider
sections.content: Veronica, this is Mark. Let me just build on Grand Island. And I'm asking everyone for understanding if we can't go into much more detail than what we're already sharing, I do hope everyone appreciates we're already super transparent about this, both in our presentation and this call. In particular, given the fact that we're right in the middle of the response period. The audit was concluded on October 24. You typically have 15 working days to respond to this. So when we're in the middle of that, and hence, it's pretty hard at this point to derive any definite conclusions. While certainly, everyone would prefer to see 0 observations, and we're continuously striving to have 0 observations, it's also a fact that this is very rare these days. And it's actually quite common that FDA audits do get concluded with a few observations of this type. When it comes to these observations, it's important that our earlier statement still applies, and that is I think they can be fully addressed without curtailing any output at the plant, and also inside the FDA remediation cost envelope that we have outlined to you in the earlier calls. So in a sense, I think the development overall, building on the audit in 2011, the one that was concluded in early 2013 and the one that was concluded now, we're very pleased with the development, and as I said, quite optimistic about getting to VAI status.

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: Okay. And Stephan, can I just quickly follow-up? Because I think previously, you had said the historical margin guidance for Kabi was 18 to 21, and I think then you sort of modulated to say, well, it's probably not 20 to 21 but it could be 18 to 20. So are you still comfortable with that as the medium-term outlook for the business?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Nice try, Veronica. To recap what I was saying, was 20 to 21 was the cream -- was the drug shortage cream on top and that didn't apply. And that, I also said that the 18 to 20 were diluted by stronger medical devices business, in particular post the arrival of Fenwal. And that therefore, like-for-like, we're probably now looking at a 17 to 19. My recollection is that, that is what I said as part of the third quarter [ph].

sections.speaker: Veronika Dubajova
sections.content: And you're happy with that statement, still?

sections.speaker: Stephan Sturm
sections.content: Veronica, again, medium-term guidance in 2004 (sic) [2014], that will be in February 2015. And as I said, I do get the sense that you're concerned about a step down from that level, and I don't share that concern.

sections.speaker: Martin Brunninger
sections.content: I have 2, 1 HELIOS and 1 Kabi. I'll start with HELIOS. You said the DRG-inflator or [indiscernible] was increased by 2.53%.
